zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#92168.body:
### Problem Description

Microchip is adding SoC support for multiple device families within Zephyr, including devices based on various architectures such as ARM, MIPS, and other proprietary platforms. Our goal is to deliver unified architecture for all Microchip-related content.

To achieve this, we recognize the need to standardize SoC and driver support, establishing a scalable foundation for integrating additional devices in the future. This approach will also help minimize redundant source code.

### Proposed Change (Summary)

By standardizing the following areas, we aim to ensure alignment among all contributors.

- Organization of Microchip SoCs
- Peripheral Driver Grouping
- Device Header files

### Proposed Change (Detailed)

1	Organization of Microchip SoCs
=====================================
1.1	Problem description
-----------------------
Currently, Microchip devices are distributed across both the **atmel** and **microchip** directories. The existing directory structure is as follows:

```
soc/atmel
├── sam
│   ├── sam3x
│   ├── sam4e
│   ├── samv71
│   └── same70
└─── sam0
    ├── samc20
    ├── samd21
    ├── samd51
    └─── same54
```
The current grouping of SoCs and drivers under 'sam' and 'sam0' does not align with Microchip/Atmel’s official device naming conventions. Additionally, this structure can be confusing, as it may be mistaken for the supported architecture (e.g., Cortex-M0+).

Introducing new device families—from **SAM**, **PIC32C**, **PIC32M**, **WBZ**, and **dsPIC**—into the current hierarchy would likely increase confusion for end users. A more standardized and intuitive directory structure is needed to improve clarity and maintainability as support for additional device families is added.

1.2	Proposed Change
-------------------
We recommend grouping devices according to their respective families, as reflected in the Microchip portal. We propose adopting the following organizational structure for device support.

SoC Organization
-------------------
```
soc/microchip
├── sam
│   ├── sam_d5x_e5x
│   │   ├── samd51
│   │   └── same53
│   ├── sam_e7x_s7x_v7x
│   │   ├── same70
│   │   └── samv71
│   └── sam_c2x
│
├── pic32c
│   ├── pic32cx_bz
│   ├── pic32cx_sg
│   ├── pic32cm_jh
│   └── pic32cz_ca
│ 
├── pic32m
│   ├── pic32mx
│   ├── pic32mz_ef
│   └── pic32mz_w
│
├── dspic33
│   ├── dspic33a
│   └── dspic33c
│
└─── wbz
    ├── wbz35x
    │   ├── wbz350
    │   └── wbz351
    └─── wbz45x
```

Boards Organization
-------------------
```
boards/microchip
├── sam
│   ├── sam_e54_xpro
│   ├── sam_e54_cult
│   └── sam_v71_xult
│
├── pic32c
│   ├── pic32cx_sg41_cult
│   ├── pic32cz_ca80_cult
│   ├── pic32cm_jh01_cpro
│ 
├── pic32m
│   ├── pic32mz_ef_sk
│   ├── pic32mz_ef_curiosity
│
├── dspic33
│   ├── dspic33a_curiosity
│   └── dspic33ch_curiosity
│
└─── wbz
    ├── wbz451_curiosity
```

2	Peripheral Driver Grouping
==============================
2.1	Problem description
-----------------------
Currently, Microchip peripheral drivers are grouped under `sam` and `sam0`. However, Microchip offers a wide range of variants for peripherals, many of which differ significantly and share few commonalities.

Organizing peripheral drivers solely by device name (e.g., `sam`, `sam0`, `pic32`) is not scalable, as many peripheral variants are shared across multiple SoC platforms, including PIC32C, PIC32M, SAM, and wireless devices. A more flexible and scalable approach is needed to accommodate the diverse and overlapping nature of peripherals across Microchip’s product families.

2.2	Proposed Change
-------------------
Peripheral drivers should be created for each specific peripheral variant following the Zephyr driver structure. We recommend using the following naming convention for clarity, since multiples drivers can be available for same peripheral type:
`<zephyr peripheral name>_mchp_<device peripheral name>_g<group number>.c`
'g' stands for "group." The group number is used to organize peripheral drivers for different IP versions. Similar IPs can be assigned the same group number to share a common driver, while other sets of peripherals can be grouped under a different group number as appropriate.

Examples : `pinctrl_mchp_pps_g1.c`, `adc_mchp_g3.c`

Binding file naming : `microchip,<device peripheral name>-g<group number>-<zephyr driver name>.yaml`
For example, `microchip,sercom-g1-spi.yaml`, `microchip,rtc-g4.yaml`

- Peripheral drivers should be reused across all devices that utilize the same peripheral variant. This will be achieved using the well defined devicetree bindings.
- Additionally, similar peripheral variants should be grouped together to minimize redundant code and improve maintainability.
- if `device peripheral name` and `zephyr driver name` is same, avoid `zephyr driver name` field.
- The hardware peripheral IPs supported by each group (e.g., g1) must be specified in the corresponding group driver binding file.
- The hardware IPs supported by a Device must be specified in the SoC device family and series Kconfig files.
- Exceptions may exist for certain peripherals, such as `clock`, where the implementation involves multiple hardware IPs and is specific to a particular device family. In such cases, the driver may be named after the device family. For example: `clock_mchp_sam_d5x_e5x.c`.

3	Usage of ASF Header files
=============================
3.1	Problem description
-----------------------

This problem is only applicable for peripheral drivers that rely on header files from ASF. 
Such drivers presents the following challenges:

- Not all ASF header files are licensed under Apache 2.0, which may cause licensing conflicts.
- ASF does not support newer devices, limiting driver reusability and compatibility with the latest hardware.
- Creates the need for fixup files

3.2	Proposed Change
-------------------
Start to use the [Microchip Device Family Packs (DFPs)](https://packs.download.microchip.com). The DFPs are licensed under Apache 2.0 and provide comprehensive support for all new Microchip devices. This approach ensures both license compliance and compatibility with the latest hardware. The DFPs code will reside inside Microchip specific HAL.

The fixups entries should be avoided from now on. The current fixups entries should be reworked inside Microchip HAL and Zephyr mainline if existent. The Atmel fixups still an exception and should be migrated on a second opportunity. New entries on Atmel should follow this new proposal. With this new directive it is expected that code will be cleaner, less confuse and decrease the maintenance.

### Dependencies

Impact 
=====

- All newly added SoC, board, and driver files will adhere to the new organizational structure, driver grouping methodology, and will utilize DFP header files.
- SAME5x/D5x SoCs currently under the Atmel directory will be migrated to the new organization, adopt the updated driver grouping, and transition to using DFP header files.
- Support for all other Microchip and Atmel devices will remain unchanged at this time.

### Concerns and Unresolved Questions

_No response_

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#92397.body:
### Describe the bug

According to the board porting guide (here referencing v3.7.0 but surely the same on latest Zephyr),
https://docs.zephyrproject.org/3.7.0/hardware/porting/board_porting.html

```
Only one .dts file will be used, and the most specific file which exists will be used.

This means that if both plank.dts and plank_soc1_foo.dts exist, then when building for plank / plank/soc1, then plank.dts is used. When building for plank//foo / plank/soc1/foo the plank_soc1_foo.dts is used.

This allows board maintainers to write a base devicetree file for the board or write specific devicetree files for a given board’s SoC or variant.
```

What I understood from that is that conversely if **only** `plank.dts` exists (base devicetree file for the board) then it will be used for **all** `plank`, `plan/soc1`, `plank//foo` or even `plan/soc1/foo` allowing effectively board maintainers to **inherit/share elegantly** a common device tree structure for **their board variants**, adjusting it for instance with a different defconfig or with an overlay only. 

But it is not the case and building for `plank//foo` in that case actually fallback to the `common/empty/stub.dts` unexpectedly

Here is the related code fron dts.cmake
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/v3.7.0-rc3/cmake/modules/dts.cmake#L126-L144

```cmake
if(NOT DEFINED DTS_SOURCE)
  zephyr_build_string(board_string SHORT shortened_board_string
                      BOARD ${BOARD} BOARD_QUALIFIERS ${BOARD_QUALIFIERS}
  )
  if(EXISTS ${BOARD_DIR}/${shortened_board_string}.dts AND NOT BOARD_${BOARD}_SINGLE_SOC)
    message(FATAL_ERROR "Board ${ZFILE_BOARD} defines multiple SoCs.\nShortened file name "
            "(${shortened_board_string}.dts) not allowed, use '<board>_<soc>.dts' naming"
    )
  elseif(EXISTS ${BOARD_DIR}/${board_string}.dts AND EXISTS ${BOARD_DIR}/${shortened_board_string}.dts)
    message(FATAL_ERROR "Conflicting file names discovered. Cannot use both "
            "${board_string}.dts and ${shortened_board_string}.dts. "
            "Please choose one naming style, ${board_string}.dts is recommended."
    )
  elseif(EXISTS ${BOARD_DIR}/${board_string}.dts)
    set(DTS_SOURCE ${BOARD_DIR}/${board_string}.dts)                    <<<<<< plank_soc1_foo.dts is accepted
  elseif(EXISTS ${BOARD_DIR}/${shortened_board_string}.dts)
    set(DTS_SOURCE ${BOARD_DIR}/${shortened_board_string}.dts)  <<<<<< plank_foo.dts is accepted
  endif()
endif()
```

I guess a last fallback (the least specific) is missing? Or what is the reason for not accepting it?

```cmake
  elseif(EXISTS ${BOARD_DIR}/${BOARD}.dts)
    set(DTS_SOURCE ${BOARD_DIR}/${BOARD}.dts)  <<<<<< plank.dts should be accepted as well?
  endif()
```

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Annoyance – Minor irritation; no significant impact on usability or functionality.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#92798.body: ### Describe the bug I'm on commit hash: can_mcux_flexcan.c lacks proper bounds checking to ensure that send calls don't overrun outside of the area allocated by the flexcan peripheral for message buffers. On line 737 of can_mcux_flexcan.c inside of mcux_flexcan_send, there is this section of code: ``` for (alloc = 0; alloc < MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_TX; alloc++) { if (!atomic_test_and_set_bit(data->tx_allocs, alloc)) { break; } } data->tx_cbs[alloc].function = callback; data->tx_cbs[alloc].arg = user_data; xfer.mbIdx = ALLOC_IDX_TO_TXMB_IDX(alloc); ``` As you can see, the driver simply iterates from 0 to `MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_TX`. `MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_TX` is set by the following: ``` #define MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_TX (MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_MB - MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_RX) ``` ``` #define MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_MB FSL_FEATURE_FLEXCAN_HAS_MESSAGE_BUFFER_MAX_NUMBERn(0) ``` ``` #define FSL_FEATURE_FLEXCAN_HAS_MESSAGE_BUFFER_MAX_NUMBERn(x) (64) ``` ``` #define MCUX_FLEXCAN_MAX_RX (CONFIG_CAN_MAX_FILTER + RX_START_IDX) ``` However, if the peripheral is configured to use 64 byte message buffers, a maximum of 64 buffers is wrong. From the datasheet, the max message buffer would be 13: Additionally, even if this max were set correctly, it doesn't seem great to just assume we will have an open buffer. In the case of no open buffer, this code just assumes the last one as the buffer to use. Really, this code should error out if it can't find an open buffer. Regardless, `alloc` ends up being set incorrectly and eventually triggers a data bus error within the `FLEXCAN_SetFDTxMbConfig` call when it tries to access memory outside of the message buffer space. In my case, this is always when it hits mailbox 14. It's probably unlikely to hit this if your CAN bus is operating normally as you won't reach the higher mailbox numbers. However, when the CAN peripheral is in an error state, the mailboxes just stack up until you overflow. ### Regression - [ ] This is a regression. ### Steps to reproduce Enable CAN FD on the can_mcux_flexcan.c driver, put the CAN bus into an error state so that mailboxes are not being cleared (not strictly necessary, but makes it easier to hit this case), send messages until mailboxes overflow. Edit: I'm not sure the error state is actually a part of this or would actually work. I think I'm overflowing just through normal operation. ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### Impact Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable. ### Environment Commit: 0285cf4235cec506e0c7d4e1f9e63d2de5fca783 Device: Custom IMXRT1064 board ### Additional Context _No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#92798.title: can: can_mcux_flexcan.c allows a mailbox overrun resulting in an imprecise data bus fault

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93012.body: ### Describe the bug The current implementation of the `regulator-fixed` read the current state of the GPIO before writing and only changes the state of that GPIO, when it is different from the expected state. This leads to the problem, that a requested new state is not applied when a wrong value is being returned by `gpio_pin_get_dt()` and the voltage change is not effective. I tried to use this driver with a nRF9151 and noticed, that it somehow did lock up in one specific state. The nRF9151 does actually disconnect the input buffer when configured as output. This can also be seen on the diagram of how the pins on a nRF9151 work. The nRF9151 allows to enable the input driver while being configured as output. The driver expects in this case that `gpio_pin_configure()` the `GPIO_OUTPUT` and `GPIO_INPUT` flags are being set. To allow the `regulator-gpio` driver to work with nordic micro controllers, i see two possible improvements, which would solve this: - Don't check the current state of the GPIO before setting it, or allow to disable this behavior - Set `GPIO_OUTPUT` and `GPIO_INPUT` flags when configuring the control GPIOs ### Regression - [ ] This is a regression. ### Steps to reproduce - Connect a GPIO controlled voltage regulator to a nordic micro controller(or any micro controller not allowing read back from outputs by default) - Configure the voltage regulator as `regulator-gpio` in the device tree - Change the requested voltage e.g. via the regulator-shell and observe, that certain voltages won't change even when requested ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### Impact Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable. ### Environment - MCU: nRF9151 - SDK: nRF Connect SDK 3.0.1 ### Additional Context _No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93494.body:
**Describe the bug**
below case are failed

- drivers.audio.dmic_api.dmic.bad_pair
- drivers.audio.dmic_api.dmic.max_channel
- drivers.audio.dmic_api.dmic.stereo_channel
- sample.drivers.i2s.codec
- testcase path is samples/drivers/audio/dmic

test is failed on v4.2.0-415-g1f69b91e909c on mimxrt595_evk/mimxrt595s/cm33
testcase path is tests/drivers/audio/dmic_api

git bisect found 1f69b91e909cc37301ac15d7e7f30e87c413ab21 is the first bad commit

```
commit 1f69b91e909cc37301ac15d7e7f30e87c413ab21
Author: Mike J. Chen <mjchen@google.com>
Date:   Thu May 29 17:17:03 2025 -0700

    drivers: audio: dmic_mcux: remove arbitrary mapping of pdm to dmic channel

    The dmic_mcux driver required a mapping of paired dmics to
    specify the same pdm number, and would arbitrarily assign the left
    channel of the pair to even dmic channel number and the right
    to an odd dmic channel number.

    Change this so that the pdm number in the mapping is
    used as the dmic channel number, and paired dmics are checked to
    specify consecutive pdm numbers (instead of the
    same pdm number).

    This allows users to control explicitly which dmic
    channel to use and whether that dmic channel is left or right,
    without this arbitrary indirect mapping. This is important
    in case they want a dmic that is wired to be right channel
    to be assigned to dmic channel 0, which is the only channel
    that supports hwvad.

    Signed-off-by: Mike J. Chen <mjchen@google.com>

 drivers/audio/dmic_mcux.c | 15 ++++-----------
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93545.body: ### Describe the bug Hello, I would like to report a critical issue in ARM64 SMP environment. This issue started from the following Q&A. **Title: Problems SMP an new SoC(ARM64: Cortex-A53) based board. #92278** The problematic part is the _isr_wrapper function in **arch/arm64/core/isr_wrapper.S**. In line 81 of the figure, the range of allowed irq numbers is loaded into the x1 register and compared with x0. Assuming that 'CONFIG_NUM_IRQS' is 256, the value loaded into x1 will be 255. The value of the x0 register is the value of the GICC_IAR register. GICC_IAR returns CPUID in [12:10] and InterruptID in [9:0]. For example, if the CPUID of the enabled interrupt is 0 and the InterruptID is 2, the value returned in x0 is 2. In this case, since the result of cmp x0, x1 is x0 < x1, the branch to 'spurious_continue' is ignored and isr-handler is called. However, if the CPUID of the enabled interrupt is 1 and the InterruptID is 2, the value returned in x0 is 1026. In this case, since the result of cmp x0, x1 is x0 > x1, isr-handler is not called and the branch to 'spurious_continue' is called. This causes the IPI(SGI)-Handler to be called only for CPU0, which causes a scheduling deadlock. I modified it to backup x0 as follows and compare only InterruptID with x1. The reason for backing up x0 is that CPUID should be included in the value that should be written as 'GICC_EOIR' in the arm_gic_eoi function called on line 105. I verified that SMP TESTSUITE runs normally as shown in 'Relevant log output' by modifying the code and enabling 'TICKET_SPINLOCKS' option. Please review and apply it. (Since it was tested in a gicv2 environment, it is necessary to confirm that it works normally in gicv3.) Thank you. **※Note** I also attach my kconfig and devicetree files. [zephyr.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21377458/zephyr.zip) ### Regression - [ ] This is a regression. ### Steps to reproduce _No response_ ### Relevant log output ```shell NOTICE: DRAM_FREQ: 1866 [MHz] NOTICE: EXT OSC : 24 MHz NOTICE: DDR DENSITY : 2048 MB NOTICE: BL31: v2.10.4(release):v2.7.0-3028-g9fa250ea9-dirty NOTICE: BL31: Built : 17:22:41, Jul 22 2025 NOTICE: APACHE5 SIP SVC Version 0.8 NOTICE: verify header done - rsd: 2, sclk: 67000000, pol(H)-pha(H), quad NOTICE: leave nc_loader_read_and_verify_header... zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93668.body:
### Describe the bug

Just confirmed with zephyr ref 2a88cb50f57 on my custom esp32s3 hardware:

Since [PR 91027](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/91027) was merged, the ledc pwm clock source selection uses RC_FAST automatically. Since I don't have the I2C drivers enabled and thus preventing `periph_rtc_dig_clk8m_enable()` from ever getting called, the line 162 `channel->clock_src_hz = periph_rtc_dig_clk8m_get_freq();` sets clock_src_hz to zero in `pwm_led_esp32_timer_config()`.
This makes the pwm code print errors to the log and prevents it from working.

My current workaround is to patch `components/esp_hw_support/clk_ctrl_os.c` like this:

```
diff --git a/components/esp_hw_support/clk_ctrl_os.c b/components/esp_hw_support/clk_ctrl_os.c
index 14ba0446d1..7a9989fb26 100644
--- a/components/esp_hw_support/clk_ctrl_os.c
+++ b/components/esp_hw_support/clk_ctrl_os.c
@@ -46,6 +46,11 @@ uint32_t periph_rtc_dig_clk8m_get_freq(void)
     /* Workaround: CLK8M calibration cannot be performed, we can only return its theoretic value */
     return SOC_CLK_RC_FAST_FREQ_APPROX;
 #else
+#if SOC_CLK_RC_FAST_SUPPORT_CALIBRATION
+    if (s_rc_fast_freq == 0) {
+        s_rc_fast_freq = esp_clk_tree_rc_fast_get_freq_hz(ESP_CLK_TREE_SRC_FREQ_PRECISION_EXACT);
+    }
+#endif
     return s_rc_fast_freq;
 #endif
 }
```

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. compile the blinky pwm sample with logging on zephyr version >= v4.2.0 on any esp32s3 board
2. start debugging with espressif monitor
3. observe the error messages and the non-working pwm

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93779.body: ### Describe the bug After porting our application from v4.1 to v4.2, the video device stopped working. This application is using a video device, ZBus and some C++ libraries that has exceptions. It's running on a custom board with a STM32H747. I managed to reproduce the issue with the Arduino Nicla Vision on the sample `zephyr/samples/subsys/usb/uvc` by adding a ZBus channel and using C++ exceptions. The sample will crash depending on the number of ZBus channels defined. ### Regression - [x] This is a regression. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Add a C++ file that uses exceptions `zephyr/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/src/test.cpp`: ```c++ extern "C" { void test() { try{ throw -1; } catch (int e) { return; } } } ``` 2. Edit the sample `zephyr/samples/subsys/usb/uvc` as follows: ```diff diff --git a/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/CMakeLists.txt b/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/CMakeLists.txt index 62b0a45e66a..73ee9925745 100644 --- a/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/CMakeLists.txt +++ b/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/CMakeLists.txt @@ -5,4 +5,4 @@ find_package(Zephyr REQUIRED HINTS $ENV{ZEPHYR_BASE}) project(usb_video) include(${ZEPHYR_BASE}/samples/subsys/usb/common/common.cmake) -target_sources(app PRIVATE src/main.c) +target_sources(app PRIVATE src/main.c src/test.cpp) diff --git a/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/src/main.c b/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/src/main.c index e2520556ba1..bd2d2c8b8d4 100644 --- a/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/src/main.c +++ b/samples/subsys/usb/uvc/src/main.c @@ -20,6 +20,27 @@ LOG_MODULE_REGISTER(uvc_sample, LOG_LEVEL_INF); const struct device *const uvc_dev = DEVICE_DT_GET(DT_NODELABEL(uvc)); const struct device *const video_dev = DEVICE_DT_GET(DT_CHOSEN(zephyr_camera)); + +#include + +typedef struct {int a;} dummy_t; + +ZBUS_CHAN_DEFINE(dummy_chan, dummy_t, NULL, NULL, ZBUS_OBSERVERS(my_listener), ZBUS_MSG_INIT(0)); +//ZBUS_CHAN_DEFINE(dummy_chan2, dummy_t, NULL, NULL, ZBUS_OBSERVERS(my_listener), ZBUS_MSG_INIT(0)); +//ZBUS_CHAN_DEFINE(dummy_chan3, dummy_t, NULL, NULL, ZBUS_OBSERVERS(my_listener), ZBUS_MSG_INIT(0)); +//ZBUS_CHAN_DEFINE(dummy_chan4, dummy_t, NULL, NULL, ZBUS_OBSERVERS(my_listener), ZBUS_MSG_INIT(0)); + +void listener_callback_example(const struct zbus_channel *chan) +{ + const dummy_t *acc; + if (&dummy_chan == chan) { + acc = zbus_chan_const_msg(chan); // Direct message access + LOG_DBG("From listener -> a=%d", acc->a); + } +} + +ZBUS_LISTENER_DEFINE(my_listener, listener_callback_example); + int main(void) { struct usbd_context *sample_usbd; @@ -167,5 +188,6 @@ int main(void) k_poll_signal_reset(&sig); } + test(); return 0; } ``` 3. Build and flash the sample like so: ```bash west build -b arduino_nicla_vision/stm32h747xx/m7 zephyr/samples/subsys/usb/uvc -DCONFIG_ZBUS=y -DCONFIG_CPP=y -DCONFIG_STD_CPP20=y -DCONFIG_GLIBCXX_LIBCPP=y -DCONFIG_CPP_EXCEPTIONS=y west flash ``` 4. The application will crash. 5. (Bonus) Uncomment the commented `ZBUS_CHAN_DEFINE` and flash the app. The app will work again! ### Relevant log output ```shell - ``` ### Impact Showstopper – Prevents release or major functionality; system unusable. ### Environment - OS: Linux - Toolchain: zephyr SDK 0.17.2 - Version: v4.2.0 ### Additional Context When checking the generated `zephyr.map` file, it seems that the `video_device_area` load address isn't aligned by 4. It's just taking the same address as the previous symbol `.tm_clone_table`. So when adding 3 more ZBus channels, it will properly align the `video_device_area`. ``` zbus_channel_observation_mask_area 0x0000000024000ebc 0x1 load address 0x000000000805d3d8 0x0000000024000ebc _zbus_channel_observation_mask_list_start = . *(SORT_BY_NAME(SORT_BY_ALIGNMENT(._zbus_channel_observation_mask.static.*))) ._zbus_channel_observation_mask.static.dummy_chan00_mask_ 0x0000000024000ebc 0x1 app/libapp.a(main.c.obj) 0x0000000024000ebc dummy_chan00_mask 0x0000000024000ebd _zbus_channel_observation_mask_list_end = . .gcc_except_table *(SORT_BY_ALIGNMENT(.gcc_except_table) SORT_BY_ALIGNMENT(.gcc_except_table.*)) .tm_clone_table 0x0000000024000ec0 0x0 load address 0x000000000805d3d9 *(SORT_NONE(EXCLUDE_FILE(*crtend.o) .tm_clone_table)) .tm_clone_table 0x0000000024000ec0 0x0 /home/dan/zephyr-sdk-0.17.2/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/thumb/v7e-m/nofp/crtbegin.o *(SORT_NONE(.tm_clone_table)) .tm_clone_table 0x0000000024000ec0 0x0 /home/dan/zephyr-sdk-0.17.2/arm-zephyr-eabi/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-zephyr-eabi/12.2.0/thumb/v7e-m/nofp/crtend.o 0x0000000024000ec0 __TMC_END__ video_device_area 0x0000000024000ec0 0x30 load address 0x000000000805d3d9 0x0000000024000ec0 _video_device_list_start = . *(SORT_BY_NAME(SORT_BY_ALIGNMENT(._video_device.static.*))) ._video_device.static.dcmi_ 0x0000000024000ec0 0x10 zephyr/drivers/video/libdrivers__video.a(video_stm32_dcmi.c.obj) ._video_device.static.gc2145_0_ 0x0000000024000ed0 0x10 zephyr/drivers/video/libdrivers__video.a(gc2145.c.obj) ._video_device.static.uvc0_ 0x0000000024000ee0 0x10 zephyr/subsys/usb/device_next/libsubsys__usb__device_next.a(usbd_uvc.c.obj) 0x0000000024000ef0 _video_device_list_end = . ``` Edit: I ran some tests using the debugger probe to inspect the values of video_device_area in our custom application. In our case, the data of video_device_area was unexpectedly shifted by 2 bytes. The screenshot below shows: - The relevant section from the zephyr.map file (top) - The loaded value of video_device_area and its expected value (bottom left) - The memory dump of that region in RAM (bottom right) ### Workaround The following patch seem to fix the issue: ```diff diff --git a/include/zephyr/linker/cplusplus-ram.ld b/include/zephyr/linker/cplusplus-ram.ld index d4314994919..77b5183d5b6 100644 --- a/include/zephyr/linker/cplusplus-ram.ld +++ b/include/zephyr/linker/cplusplus-ram.ld @@ -11,7 +11,7 @@ } GROUP_DATA_LINK_IN(RAMABLE_REGION, ROMABLE_REGION) #if defined (CONFIG_CPP_EXCEPTIONS) - SECTION_PROLOGUE(.tm_clone_table,,) + SECTION_DATA_PROLOGUE(.tm_clone_table,,) { KEEP (*(SORT_NONE(EXCLUDE_FILE (*crtend.o) .tm_clone_table))) KEEP (*(SORT_NONE(.tm_clone_table))) ```
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93779.title: Flash alignment issue

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#93881.body:
### Describe the bug

According to the [docs](https://docs.zephyrproject.org/apidoc/latest/group__i2c__interface.html#ga4cb0ae2cf41fc2105d1baa5e496e5dae), a negative return from `i2c_target_read_requested_cb_t` or `i2c_target_read_processed_cb_t` should:

> cause the controller to ignore bus operations until a new start condition is received.

However, what actually happens is that the I2C target stops responding, not quite sure what happens, voltage turns off and break points don't get hit anymore.

Here's a normal test with these functions returning 0:
```
I2C> scan
I2C address search:
0x60 (0xC0 W) (0xC1 R)

I2C> [0xC1 r:1]

I2C START
TX: 0xC1 ACK 
RX: 0x42 NACK 
I2C STOP
I2C> [0xC1 r:1]

I2C START
TX: 0xC1 ACK 
RX: 0x42 NACK 
I2C STOP
I2C> [0xC1 r:1]
```

Here's a test with `sample_target_read_processed_cb` returning `-1`:
```
I2C> [0xC1 r:1]

I2C START
TX: 0xC1 ACK 
RX: 0x42 NACK 
I2C STOP
I2C> [0xC1 r:1]

I2C START
TX: 0xC1 NACK 
RX: 0xFF NACK 
I2C STOP
```

FWIW I'm testing with an Arduino Giga R1.

Also, this might be related: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/80994

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. In `samples/drivers/i2c/custom_target/src/main.c` change `sample_target_read_processed_cb` to return `-1`.
2. Read from the I2C target.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#94352.body:
### Summary

The current implementation of the the callback for supported audio contexts in the BAP unicast client does nearly nothing: 
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/main/subsys/bluetooth/audio/bap_unicast_client.c#L4227-L4266

### Describe the solution you'd like

The function should implement subscription of the characteristic as well as provide the read supported audio context to the upper layer via a `supported_contexts` similar to the `available_contexts` callback: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/main/include/zephyr/bluetooth/audio/bap.h#L1777-L1778

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#94559.body:
### Describe the bug

The [doc](https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/boards/amd/versalnet_rpu/doc/index.html#programming-and-debugging) for Versal NET RPU says:

> Build and flash in the usual way. Here is an example for the Hello World application.
> 
> ```
> # From the root of the zephyr repository
> west build -b versalnet_rpu samples/hello_world
> west flash
> ```
> 
> You should see the following message on the console:
> ```
> Hello World!
> ```

But it doesn't work. This is due to [this](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/92464/commits/7fcc43d05ab2fe71ab4f36f134c64f61ee5c14b4) change:


### Regression

- [x] This is a regression.

1. version
2. update
3. build
4. flash

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

We have an TFM ITS backend for the Settings subsystem https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/commit/cba074230d874eb0f7a01b8ebe96d95c11e10c8a we should also create a TFM PS backend.
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/87778



### Describe the solution you'd like

Extend the effort of providing secure persistent storage of settings in Zephyr by creating a settings_ps backend allowing the settings subsystem running on a non-secure target running with TFM to access disk.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#94857.body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

ZBus has no support for a multi-core scenario. When you have more than one core in an MCU (AMP, case), you should have two isolated instances of zbus, one on each core. There is no straightforward method to synchronize some channels across zbus instances. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

The initial solution should involve creating a special kind of channel called a shadow channel, which will reflect the message on other cores. This is a one-way synchronization only. There will be one core that owns the original channel, and the other core will see that as read-only. To make this work, we will need a proxy agent responsible for communicating with other proxy agents across the cores to keep the shadow channels synchronized. 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Manual synchronization, using an inter-processor communication mechanism to synchronize channels across zbus instances.
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#94857.title: ZBus multi-core support

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95081.body:
### Describe the bug

There is a race condition in the handling of thread restoration due to meta IRQs on SMP.

The attached test case can show the erroneous behavior.

[meta-irq.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22012822/meta-irq.zip)

Meta IRQ threads are allowed to preempt a cooperative thread with the promise that when it finishes, it resumes the interrupted cooperative thread. This works on a UP system, but it only works most of the time on an SMP system. In an SMP system, while the meta IRQ is being processed, another CPU may hit a schedule point and schedule in that preempted cooperative thread. When the meta-IRQ thread is finished, the following can be observed.

1. The CPU that handled the meta IRQ thread can not restore the preempted cooperative thread as it is now executing on a different CPU.
2. That same CPU does not schedule in a new thread, rendering it useless.

Although an argument can be made that the 1st observation is not necessarily wrong as the cooperative thread remains a currently executing thread, it does imply that a rescheduling took place to move the thread between CPUs. As a result, neither cooperative threads nor schedule locked threads can assume that they will not be shuffled around  on an SMP system that uses meta IRQs. If this is truly what we want, then the documentation must be updated to reflect this caveat. If not, then it should be fixed.

As to the 2nd observation, this is clearly unwanted behavior.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

 1. Extract the attached project
 2. cd <project directory>
 3. west build -p -b qemu_cortex_a53/qemu_cortex_a53/smp ./ -t run
 4. Inspect the output.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

Zephyr Version: main branch

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95336.body:
### Describe the bug

The AIROC WiFi driver uses the same initialization priority as the generic WiFi configuration (CONFIG_WIFI_INIT_PRIORITY = 80), but depends on SDHC controller for SDIO communication(when using sdio bus). This creates a dependency violation:

The AIROC WiFi driver in airoc_wifi.c is defined with:
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/09a034e8ce9e56b7dd158a9c82fe73587c4eb83b/drivers/wifi/infineon/airoc_wifi.c#L880-L882
This uses CONFIG_WIFI_INIT_PRIORITY = 80, but the SDHC dependency has priority 85 on STM32, ESP32, RENESAS, etc platform, causing initialization order violation:
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/09a034e8ce9e56b7dd158a9c82fe73587c4eb83b/drivers/sdhc/Kconfig#L26-L30

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

with `west build samples/net/wifi/shell -b arduino_giga_r1/stm32h747xx/m7 -d build -p always -- -DCONFIG_HEAP_M EM_POOL_SIZE=24576` :

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95359.body:
### Describe the bug

The [spi_loopback](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/tree/main/tests/drivers/spi/spi_loopback) test fails running on frdm_rw612 when DMA is enabled and when cs_loopback is activated.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

On the FRDM-RW612 board, connect J1 pins 2 and 4 to enable SPI loopback. Connect J2 pins 2 and 6 to loop CS pin.
Using cdc3a52, build the test

```
west build -b frdm_rw612 tests/drivers/spi/spi_loopback
west flash
```
This works fine, no log attached.

### Enabling CS loopback fails

```
west build -b frdm_rw612 tests/drivers/spi/spi_loopback -- -DEXTRA_DTC_OVERLAY_FILE=/workdir/deps/zephyr/tests/drivers/spi/spi_loopback/overlay-cs-loopback-gpio.overlay
west flash
```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- zephyr-sdk-0.17.4
- https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/commit/cdc3a5264258c337cd42dd6736d8ed188c1ed23a

### Additional Context

transceive_dma doesn't seem to like having no buffer at all to transfer. This patch fix the zephyr assert.

```patch
diff --git a/drivers/spi/spi_mcux_flexcomm.c b/drivers/spi/spi_mcux_flexcomm.c
index 8a9875243db..5d3fa4662d1 100644
--- a/drivers/spi/spi_mcux_flexcomm.c
+++ b/drivers/spi/spi_mcux_flexcomm.c
@@ -657,6 +657,14 @@ static int transceive_dma(const struct device *dev,
        pm_policy_device_power_lock_get(dev);
 
        spi_context_lock(&data->ctx, asynchronous, cb, userdata, spi_cfg);
+
+       spi_context_buffers_setup(&data->ctx, tx_bufs, rx_bufs, 1);
+
+       if (data->ctx.tx_count == 0 && data->ctx.rx_count == 0)
+       {
+               ret = 0; // Not an actual error, no data to transfer
+               goto out;
+       }
 
        data->word_size_bits = word_size;
        data->word_size_bytes = (word_size > 8) ? (sizeof(uint16_t)) : (sizeof(uint8_t));
@@ -665,8 +673,6 @@ static int transceive_dma(const struct device *dev,
                goto out;
        }
 
-       spi_context_buffers_setup(&data->ctx, tx_bufs, rx_bufs, 1);
-
        spi_context_cs_control(&data->ctx, true);
 
        /* Clear FIFOs and any previous errors before transfer */
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95402.body:
### Describe the bug

After the change in commit d77b58aacb913d25c30b63620d413eaa94622750 our tool-chain is no longer working. 

We are using the GNU Arm Embedded on Windows installed in the default install directory which contains spaces. (C:\Program Files (x86)\Arm GNU Toolchain arm-none-eabi). 
This used to work fine but after updating to the latest main the following linker error appeared:

`arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find Files: No such file or directory`
`arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find (x86)/Arm: No such file or directory`
`arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find GNU: No such file or directory`
`arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find Toolchain: No such file or directory`
`arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find arm-none-eabi/14.3: No such file or directory`
`arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find rel1/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/thumb/v7e-m/nofp: No such file or directory`

I have not spotted the root cause yet but my guess is that due to the late computation of the linker path some string-replace has been missed. 
when re-installing the gnu tool-chain to a path without spaces and adjusting the environment variable the build is working again 

### Regression

- [x] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

install the gnu tool-chain in the default location (with spaces) and set the environment variables such as described here 
https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/develop/toolchains/gnu_arm_embedded.html 
compile any project using a board with an arm processor

### Relevant log output

the toolchain path is still found and set


```shell
-- Found toolchain: gnuarmemb (C:\Program Files (x86)\Arm GNU Toolchain arm-none-eabi\14.3 rel1)
... 
-- Found GnuLd: C:/Program Files (x86)/Arm GNU Toolchain arm-none-eabi/14.3 rel1/arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe (found version "2.44.0")
-- The C compiler identification is GNU 14.3.1
-- The CXX compiler identification is GNU 14.3.1
-- The ASM compiler identification is GNU
-- Found assembler: C:/Program Files (x86)/Arm GNU Toolchain arm-none-eabi/14.3 rel1/bin/arm-none-eabi-gcc.exe
```
but the linker command no longer works

```shell
C:/PROGRA~2/ARMGNU~1/142BCE~1.3RE/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/../../../../arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find Files: No such file or directory
C:/PROGRA~2/ARMGNU~1/142BCE~1.3RE/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/../../../../arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find (x86)/Arm: No such file or directory
C:/PROGRA~2/ARMGNU~1/142BCE~1.3RE/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/../../../../arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find GNU: No such file or directory
C:/PROGRA~2/ARMGNU~1/142BCE~1.3RE/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/../../../../arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find Toolchain: No such file or directory
C:/PROGRA~2/ARMGNU~1/142BCE~1.3RE/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/../../../../arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find arm-none-eabi/14.3: No such file or directory
C:/PROGRA~2/ARMGNU~1/142BCE~1.3RE/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/../../../../arm-none-eabi/bin/ld.bfd.exe: cannot find rel1/bin/../lib/gcc/arm-none-eabi/14.3.1/thumb/v7e-m/nofp: No such file or directory
collect2.exe: error: ld returned 1 exit status
ninja: build stopped: subcommand failed.
```

### Impact

### Environment

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95477.body:
### Describe the bug

On native posix platforms, the whole `zephyr/lib/posix/` subdirectory is ignored if building natively:
```cmake
if(NOT CONFIG_NATIVE_LIBC)
add_subdirectory(posix)
endif()
```
This includes the `zephyr/lib/posix/options/getopt` subdirectory which implements a non-standard (?) getopt interface, including the structure `struct getopt_state`, which is referenced when `CONFIG_SHELL_GETOPT=y` is enabled, generating errors like:
```
/home/jaagup/projects/forest/zephyr/include/zephyr/shell/shell.h:867:22: error: field has incomplete type 'struct getopt_state'
  867 |         struct getopt_state getopt;
      |                             ^
/home/jaagup/projects/forest/zephyr/include/zephyr/shell/shell.h:867:9: note: forward declaration of 'struct getopt_state'
  867 |         struct getopt_state getopt;
      |
```

On native posix, the system <getopt.h> is included, which does not define this structure.

I am open to making a PR with some direction of how best work around this conflict.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Annoyance – Minor irritation; no significant impact on usability or functionality.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95543.body:
### Describe the bug

The function `bt_csip_set_member_unregister` uses a stack allocation to restore the CSIS, but the `uuid` pointers will become invalid after the function returns. 

The test for the function did not attempt different inputs for re-registering, nor performed any actions, which caused this issue to go unnoticed. 

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Add `param.lockable = !param.lockable;` to the loop of `csip_set_member_test.c:test_register`
2. Run the `csip_set_member_register` test 
3. See assert due to invalid memory 

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

Sha: f878a55d2e936f8b4ac3f1528009c040460e7a1d

### Additional Context

Ideally the entire register and unregister should be reworked. 
At the time of this report, the register function starts from a full service declaration and removes unused characteristics. 
This could/should be modified to start with the mandatory services, and then add the wanted optional characteristics. 
The point of this would be to also optimize it a bit (less memory copy and movement), but also generally to rework it into something more readable and easier to follow. 

For this to work properly, the dynamic characteristics should/cannot use `BT_GATT_ATTRIBUTE` as that will stack allocate the UUIDs for the `bt_gatt_attr.uuid`, which need to be statically declared.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95550.body:
### Summary

The driver for TI ADS12xs0x family of ADCs statically enables the input buffers on REF pins when using an external reference voltage. TIs recommendation is to disable them if either REFP or REFN pin is close to AVDD or AVSS respectively. 

The rationale for disabling is that the buffers cannot really cope with voltages close to the rails and become non-linear, resulting in errors. https://e2e.ti.com/support/data-converters-group/data-converters/f/data-converters-forum/1453785/ads124s08-buffer-configuration/5585160

There should be a way to disable the buffers when using external reference voltages.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A devicetree boolean properties for disabling the positive and negative buffers for all channels when using external reference would be sufficient for my purposes. Removing the buffer configuration completely from the driver (i.e. use the defaults) would also solve this in my case as I'm using a low-side reference with REFN close to AVSS.

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95550.title: Allow configuring reference input buffers on ADS114s0x/ADS124s0x ADCs

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95609.body:
### Describe the bug

Raising this issue here although there's some overlap with https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/west

```
> west --version
West version: v1.4.0
```

```
> west sdk --help
usage: west sdk [-h] <subcommand> ...

List and Install Zephyr SDK

positional arguments:
  <subcommand>  select a subcommand. If omitted, treat it as the 'list' selected.
    list        list installed Zephyr SDKs
...
```
This description is incomplete: `west sdk list` will _also_ lists the available toolchain.

```
> west sdk install --help
...
-t, --toolchains toolchain_name [toolchain_name ...]
                        toolchain(s) to install (e.g. 'arm-zephyr-eabi'). If this option is not given, toolchains for all        
                        architectures will be installed. If you are unsure which one to install, install all toolchains. This    
                        requires downloading several gigabytes and the corresponding disk space.Each Zephyr SDK release may      
                        include different toolchains; see the release notes at https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/sdk-        
                        ng/releases.
```
This has a few issues:
1. A small typo (missing whitespace) "...disk space.Each..."
2. Recommending installing every toolchain feels like bad advice. The overwhelming majority of users are working on Arm, RISC-V, or  x86_64; sparc is pretty niche in 2025!
3. It feels like this command should recommend `west sdk list`.
4. `develop/west/built-in.html` doesn't mention `west sdk` at all.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

```shell

```

- Version: main

This issue was reported from the online documentation page using the 'Report an issue' button.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#95661.body:
**Important**
_This is a meta issue for maintainers and developers of relevant subsystems to track progress on USB support. Please do not ask general questions or make comments about USB support here. Please do not start a metaphysical discussion about the reasons for the existence of the host stack._

### Problem Description

Follow up on #42066, focusing on host support. For the USB device support see #95649.

Support for USB hosts in Zephyr is ramping up, but it will take some time to reach the same level as support for devices. **It is not ready for productive use. We cannot say when it will be ready, nor can we specify an exact date for the implementation of feature XYZ.** Please note that all APIs are experimental and may change at any time. Bear this in mind when working on new drivers or class drivers.

There are basic tests for both USB device and host support, which run in CI on the PR basis. We also have experimental USB/IP support, which is primarily used for exporting USB devices. There should not be any regressions, as these would also affect users of the USB device stack.

## Drivers

If you start working on the new driver, please use [MAX3421E or virtual driver](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/main/drivers/usb/uhc/uhc_max3421e.c) as a reference point and template for the new driver. Please follow the [coding style guidelines](https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/contribute/style/code.html) and keep the style consistent with the USB drivers/subsystem code. Please note that clangformat does not format code according to the coding style guidelines, so you will need to fix it afterwards.

  - [ ] New driver for Synopsys DWC2 https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/issues/102931
  - [ ] MCUX add hub topology fields and getters https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/102838

## Stack

The stack is in an experimental, early-development state. Ongoing work:

  - [x] Class (Driver) API (@josuah https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/94590)
  - [x] Support for multiple controllers (@josuah https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/100703)
  - [x] #103580
## USB Class/Function driver

There is no support for any classes or functions in the Zephyr tree. However, there is ongoing work and draft PRs on some functions. There are also no examples available yet that can be used as templates. The plan is to first add a basic test function as a counterpart to the device's loopback function, which could then be used for testing and as a template for the new class driver implementations. If you want to start work on the new class/function driver, this is the right issue to discuss. Please follow the coding style guidelines and keep the style consistent with the USB drivers/subsystem code. Please note that clangformat does not format code according to the coding style guidelines, so you will need to fix it afterwards.

  - [ ] Implementation of USB classes support
      - [ ] testusb function support as loopback counterpart
      - [ ] CDC ACM Class support (WIP @jfischer-no)
      - [x] USB Video class driver (WIP #93517 @AidenHu @josuah)
      - [ ] Audio Class 2 driver
      - [ ] HID class support
      - [ ] USB DFU class support
      - [ ] USB MSC class driver (WIP https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/issues/95661#issuecomment-3388540214 @AidenHu)
      - [ ] Bluetooth HCI function driver
      - [ ] CDC ECM function driver (WIP #99097 @santhosh-c-c #100289 @MarkWangChinese @D-Veda)
      - [ ] CDC NCM function driver (WIP @santhosh-c-c)
      - [ ] USB Serial (custom, WIP #99173 @Girinandha-M)
      - [ ] HUB function support (WIP https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/99735 @AidenHu @MarkWangChinese)

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#96070.body:
### Describe the bug

C++ exceptions are not working on my Zynq 7000 (Cortex-A9). I also suspect they may not work on 64-bit Cortex-A CPUs, since the linker scripts and MMU initialization are quite similar.

The problem is that the C++ exception handling code has its own linker sections (.ARM.extab and .ARM.exidx), but there is no corresponding entry for them in the `mmu_zephyr_ranges`. The simplest fix (although not very elegant) would be to extend the __text_region_end to include the end of the .ARM.exidx section, which I have tested.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#96265.body: ### Describe the bug I refer to article https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/boards/st/nucleo_n657x0_q/doc/index.html ~~Issue #1 : Seems that BOOT switch positions are twisted, board says that development mode is enabled :~~ ~~But article says opposite; I may be wrong here, but considered it worth noticing~~ Issue #2 : Application programming following this tutorial into external flash is not working, I didn't manage to start application from external flash WA: ~~none~~ **UPD** Having **STM32_SigningTool_CLI** in path is sufficient, booting from flash requires signed image Issue #3 Application programming following SB procedure (Serial Bootloader) is not working WA : There are couple of steps : - `export PATH=$PATH:/STMicroelectronics/STM32Cube/STM32CubeProgrammer/bin` As **STM32_SigningTool_CLI** must be available through the PATH, otherwise binary is not signed - `west build -b nucleo_n657x0_q//sb samples/basic/blinky -d build-sb` Original command is missing **-d build-sb**, otherwise it won't sign the binary - `west flash` is not working, adding `-d build-sb` doesn't help Only way around - is to program it manually: `STM32_Programmer_CLI --connect port=/dev/ttyACM0 --download build-sb/zephyr/zephyr.signed.bin 0x1 --start noack` Otherwise `west flash` tries to flash `.hex` file which is not accepted : `Error: The download command you trying to perform (--download) is missing the filePath to be loaded or it has a wrong extension, please note that the supported extension are .bin, .hex, .srec, .s19, .elf, stm32, .ext2, .ext3, .ext4, .vfat, .ubi, .jffs2, .img and .tsv files.` - In `boards/st/nucleo_n657x0_q/board.cmake` port is pointing to a wrong endpoint : `board_runner_args(stm32cubeprogrammer "--port=usb1")`; I may be misusing it, but anyways worth noticing I'll keep working on find and fix these issues on my own, but I presume article created (by ST vendor ?) Is slightly off the truth ### Regression - [ ] This is a regression. ### Steps to reproduce Follow the article and try to flash the board https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/boards/st/nucleo_n657x0_q/doc/index.html ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### Impact Not sure ### Environment Not relevant ### Additional Context _No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#96839.body:
### Summary

We have the following in LE Audio: 

`bt_has_preset_foreach`: Return either `BT_HAS_PRESET_ITER_STOP` or `BT_HAS_PRESET_ITER_CONTINUE` in the callback

and then we have the following functions that return `true` to *continue* iterating:
```c
bt_bap_base_foreach_subgroup
bt_bap_base_subgroup_foreach_bis
bt_bap_scan_delegator_foreach_state
bt_pacs_cap_foreach
bt_audio_data_parse
```

and then we have the following functions that return `true` to *stop* iterating:
```c
bt_bap_unicast_group_foreach_stream
bt_bap_broadcast_source_foreach_stream
bt_cap_unicast_group_foreach_stream
bt_cap_initiator_broadcast_foreach_stream
```

and finally we have the following functions that don't support stopping the iterating:
```c
bt_bap_unicast_server_foreach_ep
```

Additionally we have the following in the non-LE Audio Bluetooth API:

`bt_gatt_foreach_attr_type` Return either `BT_GATT_ITER_CONTINUE` or `BT_GATT_ITER_STOP` in the callback 
`bt_mesh_cdb_node_foreach` Return either `BT_MESH_CDB_ITER_CONTINUE` or `BT_MESH_CDB_ITER_STOP` in the callback 

The following functions that return `true` to *continue* iterating:
```c
bt_le_cs_step_data_parse
bt_obex_header_parse
bt_obex_tlv_parse
```

and finally we have the following functions that don't support stopping the iterating:
```c
bt_foreach_bond
bt_conn_foreach
bt_br_foreach_bond
bt_data_parse
```

We also have various functions like `bt_gatt_discover_func_t` that also rely on specified values like `BT_GATT_ITER_CONTINUE` and `BT_GATT_ITER_STOP` to continue or stop an operation. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

The LE Audio API should be consistent. Ideally all foreach or similar functions can be stopped early by the caller, to avoid unnecessary processing. 

There are currently 3 approaches to choose from:

1. `enum` return values like `BT_HAS_PRESET_ITER_STOP` and `BT_HAS_PRESET_ITER_CONTINUE`. Expandable to additional values, very explicit what the return value means. May need to define either a single 1 for LE Audio (could help with consistency), or 1 per profile/function. 
2. `true` to continue iterating. Straight forward and matches some of the host-defined ones
3. `true` to stop iterating. Only used in LE Audio it seems. 

Any of the above works, but 1. and 2. are use in host and Mesh, and thus 3. is the outlier that probably shouldn't be used. 

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#96839.title: Bluetooth: Audio: Make return values of iterating functions consistent

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#96881.body:
### Describe the bug

The current cmake logic for syscalls makes an incorrect assumption, in CMakeLists.txt
```
  # On other OS'es, modifying a file is reflected on the folder timestamp and hence detected
  # when using depend on directory level.
```
This is not correct. If I modify an existing file, the parent directory timestamp is not updated. The parent directory is only updated on file adds and removes. This is tricky to reproduce, as git checkouts and patch applies end up doing file adds even when just modifying a few lines. To reproduce, have to do a "true" file edit.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Apply this patch
1a. save to file repo.patch
```
diff --git a/drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c b/drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c
index 6ccb378e161..fb4828674a8 100644
--- a/drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c
+++ b/drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c
@@ -361,8 +361,9 @@ static int gpio_nrfx_port_clear_bits_raw(const struct device *port,
 }
 
 static int gpio_nrfx_port_toggle_bits(const struct device *port,
-                                     gpio_port_pins_t mask)
+                                     gpio_port_pins_t mask, uint32_t foo)
 {
+       (void)foo;
        NRF_GPIO_Type *reg = get_port_cfg(port)->port;
        const uint32_t value = nrf_gpio_port_out_read(reg) ^ mask;
        const uint32_t set_mask = value & mask;
diff --git a/include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h b/include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h
index 33962528136..d12bc1e8812 100644
--- a/include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h
+++ b/include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h
@@ -824,7 +824,7 @@ __subsystem struct gpio_driver_api {
        int (*port_clear_bits_raw)(const struct device *port,
                                   gpio_port_pins_t pins);
        int (*port_toggle_bits)(const struct device *port,
-                               gpio_port_pins_t pins);
+                               gpio_port_pins_t pins, uint32_t foo);
        int (*pin_interrupt_configure)(const struct device *port,
                                       gpio_pin_t pin,
                                       enum gpio_int_mode mode,
@@ -1491,10 +1491,10 @@ static inline int gpio_port_clear_bits(const struct device *port,
  * @retval -EWOULDBLOCK if operation would block.
  */
 __syscall int gpio_port_toggle_bits(const struct device *port,
-                                   gpio_port_pins_t pins);
+                                   gpio_port_pins_t pins, uint32_t foo);
 
 static inline int z_impl_gpio_port_toggle_bits(const struct device *port,
-                                              gpio_port_pins_t pins)
+                                              gpio_port_pins_t pins, uint32_t foo)
 {
        const struct gpio_driver_api *api =
                (const struct gpio_driver_api *)port->api;
@@ -1502,7 +1502,7 @@ static inline int z_impl_gpio_port_toggle_bits(const struct device *port,
 
        SYS_PORT_TRACING_FUNC_ENTER(gpio_port, toggle_bits, port, pins);
 
-       ret = api->port_toggle_bits(port, pins);
+       ret = api->port_toggle_bits(port, pins, foo);
        SYS_PORT_TRACING_FUNC_EXIT(gpio_port, toggle_bits, port, ret);
        return ret;
 }
@@ -1739,7 +1739,7 @@ static inline int gpio_pin_toggle(const struct device *port, gpio_pin_t pin)
        __ASSERT((cfg->port_pin_mask & (gpio_port_pins_t)BIT(pin)) != 0U,
                 "Unsupported pin");
 
-       return gpio_port_toggle_bits(port, (gpio_port_pins_t)BIT(pin));
+       return gpio_port_toggle_bits(port, (gpio_port_pins_t)BIT(pin), 1);
 }
 
 /**
```
1b. Apply the patch `git apply repo.patch --ignore-whitespace`
2. Cache the files to tmp
```
cp drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c /tmp/gpio_nrfx.c
cp include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h /tmp/gpio.h
```
3. Restore working directory
```
git restore drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c
git restore include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h
```
4. Do pristine build of blinky `west build -b nrf5340dk/nrf5340/cpuapp samples/basic/blinky -p always`
5. Copy in changes for "true" edit
```
cp /tmp/gpio_nrfx.c drivers/gpio/gpio_nrfx.c
cp /tmp/gpio.h include/zephyr/drivers/gpio.h
```
6. Do incremental build of blinky, see build failure `west build -b nrf5340dk/nrf5340/cpuapp samples/basic/blinky`
```
error: conflicting types for 'z_impl_gpio_port_toggle_bits'
```
7. "fix" the error by touching the parent directory `touch include/zephyr/drivers`
8. See that fix works by successfully doing an incremental build `west build -b nrf5340dk/nrf5340/cpuapp samples/basic/blinky`

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Annoyance – Minor irritation; no significant impact on usability or functionality.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97061.body:
### Summary

This task tracks the transition from C99 to C17 as the minimum required C standard version in order to compile Zephyr applications. This started originally as an RFC in #30105.

The TSC discussed this on the 30th of April 2025, the minutes can be found [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HCeTu_l1I9XXeQr9W5z1SzbzwAZaHzRLLVpFoV90Aj8/edit?tab=t.0).
An email was then [sent out](https://lists.zephyrproject.org/g/devel/message/9805) to the devel mailing list.

### Checklist

#### Toolchains

The following toolchains have been checked to verify if they provide complete or near-complete C17 support:

 - :white_check_mark: GCC (See [C language](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Standards.html#C-Language))
 - :white_check_mark: Clang/LLVM (See [C Support in Clang](https://clang.llvm.org/c_status.html))
 - :white_check_mark: IAR (See [this comment](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/issues/30105#issuecomment-2846636691))
 - :white_check_mark: Arm Toolchain for Embedded (based on Clang/LLVM, see [releases](https://github.com/arm/arm-toolchain/tags))
 - :white_check_mark: Synopsys Metaware (MWDT, based on Clang/LLVM)
 - :x: Cadence 2017 (based on GCC 4.4.x) 
 - :white_check_mark: Cadence 2022 (based on Clang/LLVM 10.x)
 
#### C standard libraries

- :white_check_mark: Minimal libc (compiles, but doesn't offer the features)
- :white_check_mark: Picolibc (confirmed by maintainer @keith-packard)
- newlib ?

### Pull Request

The PR that transitions to C17 by default is #97076
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97061.title: Checklist: Transition to C17 as the minimum required C standard version

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97249.body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Currently, the VREFBUF peripheral on STM32 devices can only be controlled via direct interaction with the STM32 HAL. This means:
- It is not represented in the devicetree.
- Clock management must be handled manually.
- Integration with other subsystems (e.g., sensors) is cumbersome.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like to use VREFBUF on STM32H5 using the regulator API.

For use cases where VREFBUF is shared with external components (such as sensors that can be powered on/off dynamically), integrating VREFBUF into the devicetree and exposing it through the regulator API would significantly improve usability. This would allow:
- Declarative configuration via devicetree.
- Automatic clock and power management.
- Easier integration with sensor drivers.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Using the STM32 HAL.
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97249.title: Add support for VREFBUF on STM32H5

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97476.body:
### Problem Description

The Shell subsystem has an API to temporarily bypass the incoming data to a callback function. This bypass callback function does not have a way to set a user context alongside the callback.

### Proposed Change (Summary)

The proposed change is to alter the `shell_set_bypass` API function to also pas a user data pointer. This user data pointer is also an argument for the bypass callback function.

This was already applied in https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/97187 but did not go through the [breaking API change](https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/develop/api/api_lifecycle.html#introducing-breaking-api-changes) steps, and is reverted in https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/97457

### Proposed Change (Detailed)

The proposed diff to the API

```diff
diff --git a/include/zephyr/shell/shell.h b/include/zephyr/shell/shell.h
index ba275c5393c..0a070d35bfc 100644
--- a/include/zephyr/shell/shell.h
+++ b/include/zephyr/shell/shell.h
@@ -789,7 +789,8 @@ typedef void (*shell_uninit_cb_t)(const struct shell *sh, int res);
  */
 typedef void (*shell_bypass_cb_t)(const struct shell *sh,
                                  uint8_t *data,
-                                 size_t len);
+                                 size_t len,
+                                 void *user_data);

 struct shell_transport;

@@ -990,6 +991,9 @@ struct shell_ctx {
        /** When bypass is set, all incoming data is passed to the callback. */
        shell_bypass_cb_t bypass;

+       /** When bypass is set, this user data pointer is passed to the callback. */
+       void *bypass_user_data;
+
        /*!< Logging level for a backend. */
        uint32_t log_level;

@@ -1364,8 +1368,9 @@ int shell_set_root_cmd(const char *cmd);
  *
  * @param[in] sh       Pointer to the shell instance.
  * @param[in] bypass   Bypass callback or null to disable.
+ * @param[in] user_data        Bypass callback user data.
  */
-void shell_set_bypass(const struct shell *sh, shell_bypass_cb_t bypass);
+void shell_set_bypass(const struct shell *sh, shell_bypass_cb_t bypass, void *user_data);

 /** @brief Get shell readiness to execute commands.
  *
```

See https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/97187

### Dependencies

_No response_

### Concerns and Unresolved Questions

_No response_

### Alternatives Considered

An alternative is to not break the API and put the user data into the shell context directly, without the coupling to the bypass function.

See https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/97467
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97476.title: RFC: Breaking API Change: Shell: Allow passing an opaque user data pointer to the bypass callback function

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97660.body:
### Problem Description

On October 15th 2025 the [joint release of Mbed TLS 4.0 and TF-PSA-Crypto 1.0 has been announced](https://lists.trustedfirmware.org/archives/list/mbed-tls@lists.trustedfirmware.org/thread/EHEP5F2C5X4HVVKGZGUQCETONNQN7H4Q/).

Many changes are included in it, some of which are of course breaking stuff with previous LTS release which is the one we currently use in Zephyr. The 2 main important ones are:
- a repo split: it won't be just Mbed TLS, but Mbed TLS (v4.0) + TF-PSA-Crypto (v1.0). The former only includes TLS and X.509 while the latter, which is a git sub-repo of the former, all the low level crypto support and they are both necessary.
  - I don't recommend flattening TF-PSA-Crypto sub-repo inside Mbed TLS because then porting patches from upstream would become harder in some cases.
- PSA API become the de-facto standard way to have access to crypto support. This allows vendor to more easily get support for their custom crypto libraries and/or support TF-M.

The standard update process for Zephyr would be:
- deprecating all the legacy crypto support that's being removed from 4.0/1.0;
- wait for the deprecation period to expire (2 releases)
- remove legacy crypto usage
- upgrade Mbed TLS.

However if we consider that in TSC it's currently being discussed to move to 2 release per year, this means that we'll have to wait for roughly a year to transition to this new Mbed TLS release. This also doesn't account for the fact that Mbed TLS is not only used in Zephyr codebase, but also in some external modules (ex: hostap, openthread) which might need some time to transition to PSA API. This would add extra delay in the adoption of the new solution.
On the other hand vendors might be interested in adopting this new release as soon as possible in order to benefit from the wider usage of PSA API.



### Proposed Change (Summary)

Introduce new Mbed TLS 4.0 + TF-PSA-Crypto 1.0 as new modules in parallel with the existing 3.6 LTS.

### Proposed Change (Detailed)

What if we add a couple of new modules to Zephyr, one for Mbed TLS 4.0 and one for TF-PSA-Crypto 1.0, which live in parallel with the current LTS support. Of course the two (LTS vs 4.0) should be mutually exclusive from a build point of view, with LTS one being the default choice for backward compatibility.
This way we'll give more time to end users and various subsystems and modules to transition to this new solution, and meanwhile the ones who would like to start benefiting from this new release can do so.

### Dependencies

Assuming the new release is added in parallel to the current one and the latter is kept as default choice for the time being, nothing should break ideally.

### Concerns and Unresolved Questions

_No response_

### Alternatives Considered

Go through normal Zephyr's deprecation + update path, but this would require much longer.
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97660.title: Add new Zephyr module for Mbed TLS 4.0 + TF-PSA-Crypto 1.0 releases

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#97674.body:
### Describe the bug

Hello,
We are attempting to run the CM7 application from HyperRAM. However, according to the `soc.c` file, the application is either executed in XIP mode or copied into ITCM, with the load address hard-coded. These configurations work fine for small applications that do not perform any other FLASH-related I/O operations.

Would it be possible to add support for running the CM7 application directly from HyperRAM? This feature would be very helpful for larger applications or use cases that require more flexible memory configurations.

Thank you for your support.


### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

* Assigning `zephyr,flash=&hyperram0`. 
* Building a dual-core sample without XIP mode:
```shell
west build -p -b mimxrt1180_evk/mimxrt1189/cm33 samples/drivers/mbox --sysbuild -- -Dremote_EXTRA_DTC_OVERLAY_FILE=$ZEPHYR_BASE/boards/nxp/mimxrt1180_evk/cm7_flash_boot.overlay

```

### Relevant log output

```shell
N/A

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

Zephyr: 4.2.99
Zephyr SDK: 0.17.4

### Additional Context

_No response_

### Describe the bug

Using Wi-Fi credentials management, with two SSIDs saved, it is not possible to connect to the first entry.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Add two Wi-Fi logins using the `wifi cred add` shell command, where the first is actually available, and the second isn't.
2. Try to connect with `wifi cred auto_connect`

```shell
uart:~$ wifi cred add -s "Working AP" -p "supersecret" -k 1
uart:~$ wifi cred add -s "Bogus AP" -p "fakesecret" -k 1
uart:~$ wifi cred auto_connect
[00:06:45.828,216] <inf> net_wifi_mgmt: Connection requested
[00:06:45.907,806] <inf> net_wifi_mgmt: Connection requested
Connection request failed (Connection timeout/3)
```

Reversing the order of adding the credentials "fixes" the issue.


### Environment

### Describe the bug

The uart bitbang callbacks (e.g. uart_bitbang_rx_counter_top_interrupt, uart_bitbang_tx_counter_top_interrupt, …) probably use the wrong device when calling nested callbacks.

For example, `uart_bitbang_rx_counter_top_interrupt`  uses dev instead of uart_dev. 

```
#ifdef CONFIG_UART_INTERRUPT_DRIVEN
				if ((data->user_cb) && (data->irq & UART_BITBANG_IRQ_PE)) {
					data->user_cb(dev, data->user_data);
				}
#endif /* CONFIG_UART_INTERRUPT_DRIVEN */
```

This causes `uart_irq_update(uart)` to always returning a value <1, because uart_irq_update is called on a counter-device instead of a uart one.
As a result, data received by the bit-banging driver is not processed correctly in the uart modem backend. This prevents, the use of the gnss subsystem (and probably of all things that make use of the uart interrupt) in combination with the bitbang driver. 





### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Build GNSS example with activated bitbang driver on a T-Beam board (v1.2). 

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

- OS: Windows
- Zephyr OS build: v4.2.0-6166-g8a72d776c5af

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#98231.body:
### Describe the bug

When using a fully qualified board (I'm using a test `native_sim/native/64/two`) my interpretation of the documentation [here](https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/hardware/porting/board_porting.html#write-kconfig-files) is that the the defconfig files should be matched with `native_sim_defconfig`, as well as each file getting more specific with each qualifier
(e.g. `native_sim_defconfig`, `native_sim_native_defconfig`, `native_sim_native_64_defconfig`, `native_sim_native_64_two_defconfig`)

> If both the common `plank_defconfig` file and one or more board qualifiers specific `plank_<qualifiers>_defconfig` files exist, then all matching files will be used. This allows you to place configuration which is common for all board SoCs, CPU clusters, and board variants in the base `plank_defconfig` and only place the adjustments specific for a given SoC or board variant in the `plank_<qualifiers>_defconfig`.

Depending on your interpretation of the documentation, the you may have a different opinion on which configs with "intermediate" qualifiers should be included... However, I would consistently expect at least `native_sim_defconfig` and `native_sim_native_64_two_defconfig` to be used regardless of interpretation of docs **and it is not**. 

Right now only `native_sim_native_64_two_defconfig` is used. I have reproduced this in a ztest test case available on a public fork of the Zephyr repo


### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Steps to reproduce (on a Linux machine):
1. Check out the branch `luskey/qualifier-maybe-bug` from https://github.com/LuskeyNoah/zephyr/ (or simply cherry pick [this commit](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/commit/55c4ed63f984af953890a21452d50b55822fd3dc))
2. Navigate to `tests/cmake/hwm/board_extend` and build for `native_sim/native/64/two` (`west build -b native_sim/native/64/two`)
3. Run the zephyr application and observe the results

### Relevant log output

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#98259.body:
### Origin

Greybus-for-zephyr: https://github.com/beagleboard/greybus-zephyr

### Purpose

Greybus was initially designed for Google's [Project Ara](https://atap.google.com/ara/) smartphone (which is [discontinued](http://www.theverge.com/2016/9/2/12775922/google-project-ara-modular-phone-suspended-confirm) now), but the first (and only) product released with it is Motorola's [Moto Mods](https://www.motorola.com/us/moto-mods).

The support for it was merged back in Linux 4.9 ([lwn article](https://lwn.net/Articles/715955/)) for use in Internet of Things applications.

### Mode of Integration

As a module: greybus

The reason for being a module is that most of the source is licensed under BSD-3-Caluse. Additionally, there are not many real users of greybus around now, so might not be worth integrating in the main tree.

### Maintainership

- @Ayush1325 
- @jadonk 
- @vaishnavachath 
- @cfriedt 

### Pull Request

_No response_

### Description

The end-goal of supporting Greybus in a microcontroller RTOS such as Zephyr is to reduce the amount of engineering effort required to write and maintain microcontroller firmware – for everyone. As a community-oriented IoT framework, we hope that translates to savings for hobbyists and industry professionals alike.

Cost distribution for an IoT Project involving both Hardware and Software. [source](https://r-stylelab.com/company/blog/iot/internet-of-things-how-much-does-it-cost-to-build-iot-solution)

Taking hardware development into consideration as well, the cost of developing an IoT App and a product can [balloon](https://r-stylelab.com/company/blog/iot/internet-of-things-how-much-does-it-cost-to-build-iot-solution) quickly, with 50% of costs going to Hardware and Integration. Firmware development lives in a fuzzy Venn-diagram area somewhere within the pie-slices of Hardware & Integration. Of that 50%, it would not be surprising that 35% is directly related to firmware development.

To mitigate the costs mentioned above, the strategy with Greybus for IoT is threefold:

- Make non-discoverable buses discoverable
- Keep the intelligence in the host
- Use standard IP-centric communication APIs

#### Discoverability

As mentioned at Linux Plumbers, most of the buses used in IoT devices are non-discoverable. That is, there is no way of knowing what buses are available on a given device, nor is it possible to know what devices are attached to the bus, without using platform-specific metadata.

In the desktop and server space, non-discoverable buses are made discoverable using the ACPI standard, passing platform-specific metadata through the BIOS or UEFI firmware.

In the embedded space, non-discoverable buses are made discoverable using Device Tree, passing platform-specific metadata through the bootloader.

With Greybus, devices are made to be discoverable via entries in the Greybus Manifest – a document that is queried as part of the Greybus communications protocol.

#### Keep the Intelligence in the Host

When we bury most of the application logic in microcontroller firmware, along with device-specific drivers, maintenance becomes costly. Updates can become infrequent and so application logic can suffer from [bit-rot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_rot). By keeping most of the application logic out of the microcontroller and in a more centralized location, updates can be better tested and deployed more frequently with greater confidence.

Also by minimizing the functionality that the microcontroller is responsible for, we reduce the attack surface area and arguably improve security.

#### Standard Communication Protocols and APIs

Greybus for IoT uses IETF, POSIX, and IEEE industry standard APIs and communication methods such as:

- TCP and UDP protocols with standard BSD Socket API calls such as connect(), send(), sendto(), recv(), and recvfrom()
- IETF standards such as [mDNS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multicast_DNS) ([RFC 6762](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6762)) and [DNS Service Discovery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zero-configuration_networking) ([RFC 6763](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6763))
- Standard POSIX calls such as open(), close(), read(), write(), and ioctl() to interact with devices over Greybus

When Greybus is integrated tightly into the host operating system kernel, as is the case for Linux, then the details of the Greybus Protocol itself actually vanish and the developer is only left with standard API calls.

**NOTE:** The content for description is taken from the following blog post: https://www.zephyrproject.org/using-linux-zephyr-greybus-for-iot/

#### Protocol support

| | Good |Partial | Broken/Missing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Audio | | | x |
| Camera | | | x |
| Component Authentication | | | x |
| Firmware Download | | x | |
| Firmware Management | | x | |
| HID | | | x |
| Lights | | x | |
| Log | x | | |
| Loopback | x | | |
| Power Supply | | | x |
| Raw | | x | |
| Vibrator | x | | |
| USB | | | x |
| GPIO | x | | |
| SPI | | x | |
| UART | | x | |
| PWM | | x | |
| I2C | x | | |
| SDIO | | | x |

* Protocols that have not received enough testing are also classified as Partial.

### Security

_No response_

### Dependencies

_No response_

### Version or SHA

a592fcab585cb23300f50d385b70e12e4c7fddce

### License (SPDX)

BSD-3-CLause
