zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103178.body:
### Describe the bug

When trying to run nsim with board `nsim/nsim_vpx5`, the runner complains:

```
Could not read properties file <ZEPHYR_BASE>/boards/snps/nsim/arc_classic/support/sim_vpx5.props
```

Notice that the props filename is missing 'n' in the beginning. This is due to `BOARD_QUALIFIERS` no longer containing '/' at the beginning, resulting in the substring command starting at the wrong character.

git bisect to this commit:

```
commit a0983a2f9192e78ff51872d1e3e8b548d333628e (HEAD)
Author: Torsten Rasmussen <Torsten.Rasmussen@nordicsemi.no>
Date:   Fri Jan 16 11:55:42 2026 +0100

    cmake: align board qualifiers in CMake with Kconfig
    
    The value os board qualifiers in CMake and Kconfig differs.
    CMake has a leading '/', as example:
    CMake:   BOARD_QUALIFIERS=/nrf52840
    Kconfig: BOARD_QUALIFIERS=nrf52840
    
    This was also discussed in
    https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/69740
    
    This commit aligns the value of the CMake variable BOARD_QUALIFIERS to
    the value of BOARD_QUALIFIERS (CONFIG_BOARD_QUALIFIERS) in Kconfig.
    
    Signed-off-by: Torsten Rasmussen <Torsten.Rasmussen@nordicsemi.no>
```

### Regression

- [x] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

`west build -p -b nsim/nsim_vpx5 samples/hello_world/ -t run`

### Relevant log output

```shell
ERROR:[NSIMDRV] Could not read properties file <ZEPHYR_BASE>/boards/snps/nsim/arc_classic/support/sim_vpx5.props
```

### Impact

Not sure

### Environment

- Zephyr SDK 0.17.4
- Commit 543fd12462f1f8c9b25d83ae9852eba34011bb90

### Additional Context

_No response_

### Describe the bug

There appear to be mistakes in haptics_handler.c it appears to be using outdated functions / includes in NCS 3.2.1:

The corrections that work for me:
`#include <zephyr/internal/syscall_handler.h>
#include <zephyr/drivers/haptics.h>

static inline int z_vrfy_haptics_start_output(const struct device *dev)
  {
        K_OOPS(K_SYSCALL_DRIVER_HAPTICS(dev, start_output));
        return z_impl_haptics_start_output(dev);
  }
  #include <zephyr/syscalls/haptics_start_output_mrsh.c>

  static inline int z_vrfy_haptics_stop_output(const struct device *dev)
  {
        K_OOPS(K_SYSCALL_DRIVER_HAPTICS(dev, stop_output));
        return z_impl_haptics_stop_output(dev);
  }
  #include <zephyr/syscalls/haptics_stop_output_mrsh.c>`

Changes made:
`#include <zephyr/syscall_handler.h> --> #include <zephyr/internal/syscall_handler.h>

Z_OOPS --> K_OOPS

Z_SYSCALL_DRIVER_HAPTICS --> K_SYSCALL_DRIVER_HAPTICS
`
Without these changes my build was failing


### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

Use haptics driver on NCS 3.2.1

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103239.body:
### Describe the bug

When an async transfer finished quickly, the internal state of the driver could come out of sync with the hardware. The tx/rx
enabled flag and PM lock was taken after starting the DMA transaction, which caused issues if the DMA complete callback
was called before the flags were updated. Set the flag and take the PM lock prior to starting the DMA transaction to avoid this.

Also release the PM rx lock upon successful completion of a transfer. The lock was previously only released when the user manually called uart_rx_disable().

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Build the uart_async_dual test with a series 2 silabs devices, it will fails to transfer correclty

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103254.body:
### Describe the bug

Sysbuild can be used to build a 3 image system for an esp32c6 or esp32c3:
   - mcuboot image for bootloader
   - "bare metal" esp-idf image for the low-power core
   - zephyr image for the high-performance core

This can be easily done using the sample code in zephyr/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp

In order to make the esp32c3/6 run as a truly low-power platform, the image that runs in the high-performance core needs to be able to call either sys_poweroff() or esp_deep_sleep_start() to power the HP core and associated peripherals down when they're not needed. Attempts to call either of these functions results in the following build error:

```
/home/dave/zephyr-sdk-0.16.8/riscv64-zephyr-elf/bin/../lib/gcc/riscv64-zephyr-elf/12.2.0/../../../../riscv64-zephyr-elf/bin/ld.bfd: zephyr/zephyr_pre0.elf section `.rtc.text' will not fit in region `lp_ram_seg'
/home/dave/zephyr-sdk-0.16.8/riscv64-zephyr-elf/bin/../lib/gcc/riscv64-zephyr-elf/12.2.0/../../../../riscv64-zephyr-elf/bin/ld.bfd: RTC_FAST segment data does not fit.
/home/dave/zephyr-sdk-0.16.8/riscv64-zephyr-elf/bin/../lib/gcc/riscv64-zephyr-elf/12.2.0/../../../../riscv64-zephyr-elf/bin/ld.bfd: region `lp_ram_seg' overflowed by 72 bytes
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
ninja: build stopped: subcommand failed.
```

Calling these functions from an esp32c6 program that _isn't_ built in the sysbuild environment does _not_ result in this build-time error. I believe that this is because the sysbuild environment tries to allocate all of the RTC SRAM to the low-power core image, whereas a non-sys build build gives the high-performance core access to RTC SRAM.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Apply the following patch to the zephyr tree:
```
diff --git a/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/prj.conf b/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/prj.conf
index 7d425a10aa2..8f1246726f4 100644
--- a/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/prj.conf
+++ b/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/prj.conf
@@ -1 +1,3 @@
 CONFIG_ULP_COPROC_ENABLED=y
+
+CONFIG_POWEROFF=y
\ No newline at end of file
diff --git a/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/src/main.c b/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/src/main.c
index 504d39c438c..b46030982b1 100644
--- a/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/src/main.c
+++ b/samples/boards/espressif/ulp/lp_core/interrupt_ulp/src/main.c
```

### Describe the bug

SiWx91x does not support concurrent read and write on the shared flash with the network coprocessor. On the M4 side (Zephyr), there are some functions that allow to manage the flash access (sli_mv_m4_app_from_flash_to_ram()) but since his function is not in RAM, it can creates a flash access itself and then creates a flash access corruption.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103339.body:
### Describe the bug

PM is handled by the network coprocessor (NWP).
There are actually two CPU states that allow runtime-idle and suspend-to-RAM.
When the CPU decides to enter the suspend-to-RAM state, it sends a request to the NWP to enter deep sleep, which will disable all high-power (HP) and ULP (ultra-low-power) peripherals. Only ultra-ultra-low-power (UULP) peripherals remain powered.

Hence the bug: sometimes we enter deep sleep without knowing whether a peripheral is in use or not. There are multiple places in the drivers where we try to take the CPU-state lock, but without consistency.
Since all drivers are linked to a power domain, something needs to be done to properly link CPU states and power-domain activity.

Moreover, when enabling PM with Bluetooth, the device crashes at boot. This is related to power-domain handling and power-save requests sent to the NWP.



### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103355.body:
### Describe the bug

Zephyr previously has used the `fixed-partitions` binding which it has inherited from Linux, this binding does not mandate the use of ranges (though does not rule it out) which means that the partitions are not mapped to the same address bus as the CPU meaning they are not memory mapped. In linux, this is not a problem because the processors likely have MMUs which mean that the address is virtual anyhow, but in zephyr this is not the case, applications for e.g. cortex M devices must be built for the absolute address that the NVM (usually flash) resides at, applications cannot be remapped and are not built with PIC, therefore the build system (and subsystems which use flash) need to know the memory mapped address of the NVM partitions to be able to use and run from them.

The traditional way of zephyr dealing with this is it cannot do it using DTS alone, the build system must have the chosen flash node set in order to get the flash base address, it then needs to have the chosen flash partition node set in order to get an offset, it then needs to add these together in order to link at the address (the alternate is manually setting the flash load address which leads to issues like area overflowing their bounds - an issue going back over 6 years https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/issues/27471 and which in itself is also error prone due to either having to specify a constant value which can get out of date with board changes, or having to use dt functions in Kconfig to add/subtract values from dt). This is the same when dealing with flash partitions, the compatible has to be read and checked, then it must go up through the parents one at a time to find one with one specific compatible which can be seen here https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/v4.3-branch/include/zephyr/devicetree/fixed-partitions.h#L80 this isn't flexible and goes against the whole idea of memory mapped devices in devicetree. This has only expanded further with the introduction of fixed-subpartitions which was introduced to allow grouping of partitions and support use-cases like having a single set of partitions which would support usage with and without a bootloader (and preventing overflowing into over partitions) or optional partition sets which can be changed using aliases/chosen nodes instead of needing to delete whole partitions then re-create them - this required parsing through yet another layer of going up parent dt nodes, though does make use of the dt `ranges` property to be relative to the parent fixed-partition node. However there is an issue with this in that for each layer, a new set of macrobatics is needed, so for fixed-subpartition inside of a fixed-subpartition, the macro in-tree will not work, it would need to have all of the inner checks which go up an additional layer of dt parentage and checking compatibles (needed because of the lack of `ranges` mapping with the `fixed-partitions` parent/grand-parent) which is unworkable for out of tree users, sometimes needing many layers, and a gigantic spread of mess to users that look at the macro and attempt to work on it.

The method for setting the flash base address and flash load offset is also echoed by how the RAM device is also picked, actually NVM devices and RAM devices _must_ be 1KiB sized and aligned due to this redirection of values, this causes wasted resources and makes some setups actually non-viable due to insufficient memory on lower end MCUs - none of which would be a problem if dt parts were used as-is without having to go through Kconfig manipulation to be viable.

Thus a way of fixing these issues is needed whereby partitions for **memory-mapped** devices are correctly described in devicetree without needing implicit knowledge of multiple layers of bindings to make sense of what their values actually mean which dt alone cannot provide. As memory mapped devices are part of the same address space as the CPU, the dt `ranges` property should be used to achieve this and zephyr should be updated to use this new system when building - the old `fixed-partitions` system should still function for non-memory mapped devices, and in a deprecated state for memory-mapped devices to allow users to update to the new system. When the dt maintainers of the linux kernel were asked about it, their responses indicate that for memory mapped devices they too agree that these should be using `ranges` to indicate that they are part of the CPU memory mapped space too as per the discussion on https://lore.kernel.org/devicetree-spec/aY_XxHMzWyJLqWog@zatzit/

One thing that likely has left to this being overlooked for so long is that a good portion of MCU devices have flash (or other) storage devices that start at 0x0, meaning that for the most part it has co-incidentally matched up with the actual offsets of the partitions anyhow.

Issue list:

- [ ] NXP @butok see https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/103222 for one board fix, please apply to all others
- [x] Nordic @nordicjm https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/103222
- [x] Raspberry pi @soburi https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/103222
- [ ] STM32 @erwango https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/103652 (in progress)

Related fixes:
- [ ] flashdisk and tests fixes: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/103631
- [ ] Legacy scheme fallback fix: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/103587

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

https://devicetree-specification.readthedocs.io/en/latest/chapter2-devicetree-basics.html#ranges

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103365.body:
### Describe the bug

As fixed in #99308, on MAX32 the "Transmit FIFO Almost Empty" interrupt is used to check for completion of a TX, but when a transfer of just one byte is initiated, the interrupt will never fire, leading to a "hang" in the serial logic that is waiting for a TX to complete before sending more.

When using the shell sample, for instance, this will manifest as shell output that suddenly stops without sending the next shell prompt. Pressing a key will raise an *RX* that will then trigger the TX to be detected as completed.

This may of course manifest in other ways, but the shell example is the most direct way to reproduce.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Build the shell sample for a MAX32655 feather board: `west build -p -b max32655fthr//m4 samples/subsys/shell/shell_module` and flash to a board
2. Connect to the console with your serial tool of choice.
3. Interact with the shell, even hitting enter at an empty prompt can trigger the bug.
4. Wait for an enter press to be received, but no new shell prompt is printed on the console

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103406.body:
### Describe the bug

Compiling for Raspberry Pi Pico, rp2040. using the arm gnu embedded toolchain, arm-none-eabi-gcc. 

Set it up following this guide: 
https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/develop/toolchains/gnu_arm_embedded.html , 

However encountered problems with compiling: 

```bash
arm-none-eabi-gcc: fatal error: cannot read spec file 'picolibc.specs': No such file or directory
compilation terminated.
```

Further up in the error message I could extract that it was from the second stage bootloader code, 
zephyr/modules/hal_rpi_pico/bootloader/CMakeLists.txt

I checked the commit history on that file and found a change from 6 months ago, 

https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/commit/3ed633b69b3a4c1d3c601e9d2636a09becb4f996

Which probably works great if you have picolibc set up, 
but what if one wishes to run it using another setup? 

**Request**: Could the CMake logic be updated to check if Picolibc is actually available/enabled before applying the spec flag, or provide documentation on how to use the GNU Arm Embedded Toolchain with this new requirement?

For now I went ahead and just reverted the changes to use nosys: 

```
target_link_options(boot_stage2 PRIVATE
  "-nostartfiles"
  # "--specs=picolibc.specs"
  "--specs=nosys.specs"
  "-T${boot_stage_dir}/boot_stage2.ld"
  )
```

And the kernel compiled, not really sure about the consequences. 

### Regression

- [x] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

CMakeLists.txt : 

```
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.20)

find_package(Zephyr REQUIRED HINTS $ENV{ZEPHYR_BASE})
project(my_pico_app)

target_sources(app PRIVATE src/main.c)
```

build.sh

```
#!/bin/bash

# 1. Set the Zephyr base path
export ZEPHYR_BASE=~/pico/pico/embeddedproject/zephyrproject/zephyr

# tell zephyr to use arm-none-eabi-gcc and not its own sdk build tool. 
# where it tries to locate its zephyr sdk. 
export ZEPHYR_TOOLCHAIN_VARIANT=gnuarmemb

export GNUARMEMB_TOOLCHAIN_PATH="/opt/arm-toolchain/arm-gnu-toolchain-14.3.rel1-x86_64-arm-none-eabi"


# 2. Source the environment (This loads the west tools/paths)
source $ZEPHYR_BASE/zephyr-env.sh

# 3. Build for the Pico 
west build -p always -b rpi_pico .
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103423.body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

## Problem                             

When using RISC-V PMP with userspace enabled, debuggers (GDB/OpenOCD) cannot access the ROM region because it's locked with PMP_L=1. This prevents setting breakpoints and reading instructions during development. In order to reproduce, you have to enable the following configs and attach a debugger:

```
CONFIG_RISCV_PMP=y
CONFIG_USERSPACE=y
```                                                                                                            
                                                                                   
## Current Behavior                                                                                                                                                 
- ROM region PMP entry is always configured with lock bit (L=1)                                                                                                     
- Debuggers running in machine mode are blocked from ROM access                                                                                                     
- Cannot set breakpoints or disassemble code during debugging

### Describe the solution you'd like

## Proposed Solution                                                                                                                                                

Add `CONFIG_PMP_UNLOCK_ROM_FOR_DEBUG` option that:
- Configures ROM region without lock bit (L=0) when enabled
- Keeps NULL pointer and stack guards locked for bug detection
- Only affects ROM region - maintains other security protections
- Clearly marked as debug-only configuration

## Use Case

Embedded development where debugging capability is essential but userspace memory protection is also needed.

## Implementation

Ready to submit PR with:
- New Kconfig option with clear help text
- Minimal code changes (only affects ROM PMP entry)
- Preserves all other PMP protections

Would this be acceptable for upstream inclusion?

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103423.title: riscv/pmp: Add config option to unlock ROM region for debugging

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103519.body:
### Describe the bug

In **Slave Half-Duplex TX** mode, the STM32 SPI driver disables SPI (SPE=0) immediately after writing data to the DR register - before the Master provides clock. Since a Slave depends on the Master for clock, data is never actually shifted out.

This affects both Interrupt and DMA transfer modes.

**Root cause**: Two code locations disable SPE unconditionally without Master/Slave distinction:

1. **`spi_stm32_complete()` (line 818)**: Calls `ll_func_disable_spi()` (SPE=0) for all non-`SPI_HOLD_ON_CS` configurations. For Slave Half-Duplex TX, data has only been *loaded* into DR - it has not yet been *shifted out* because the Master clock has not arrived.

2. **`transceive_dma()` (line 1560)**: After calling `spi_stm32_complete()`, additionally calls `LL_SPI_Disable(spi)`. Even if (1) is fixed, this second disable still forces SPE=0 in the DMA path.

**Additional symptom - Multi-byte Slave TX blocks forever**:
On STM32F4 (no FIFO, single-byte DR buffer), `spi_write()` with 2+ bytes blocks indefinitely. Byte 1 goes to the shift register, byte 2 fills DR, then TXE stays 0 because no Master clock arrives to drain the shift register. FIFO-equipped series (H7, U5) would block at higher byte counts, but the root cause is the same.

Related: #82547, #82653

**Attached**: Patch file (`spi_stm32_fix_slave_hd_tx.patch`) and reproduction test project (`spi_half_duplex_test.zip`).

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

**Hardware**: NUCLEO-F439ZI with SPI1 (Master) and SPI3 (Slave) on the same board.

**Wiring** (3 jumper wires):
```
PA5 (SPI1 SCK)  --> PC10 (SPI3 SCK)
PA7 (SPI1 MOSI) <-> PC11 (SPI3 MISO)   [Half-Duplex data line]
PA4 (GPIO CS)   --> PA15 (SPI3 NSS)     [Manual CS]
```

**Steps**:
1. Configure SPI3 as Slave, Half-Duplex
2. Call `spi_write(spi3, &slave_cfg, &tx_bufs)` with 1 byte (e.g. 0x11)
3. Observe that `spi_write()` returns immediately with SPE=0
4. Then provide Master clock from SPI1 - Master receives 0x00 (garbage), not 0x11

**Expected**: SPE should remain 1 after `spi_write()` returns. The Master should then clock out the Slave's data (0x11). Manual register control confirms this works correctly on the same hardware.

**Build**:
```bash
# Interrupt mode
west build -b nucleo_f439zi -p always -- -DCONF_FILE=prj_no_dma.conf

# DMA mode
west build -b nucleo_f439zi -p always
```

[spi_half_duplex_test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25080542/spi_half_duplex_test.zip)

### Relevant log output

```shell
Test 5 - BEFORE patch (unmodified driver, Interrupt mode):

-- Register state after spi_write() --
  CR1=0xC000  SR=0x0000
  BIDIOE=1  SPE=0  BSY=0
[CHECK] SPE
  Expected: 1 (waiting for Master clock)
  Actual:   0
  -> SPE disabled before Master clock arrived
-- Master RX attempt --
[CTRL] Master RX: 0x80 (expected 0x11)
>>> TEST 5 FAILED <<<

Test 5 - AFTER patch (slave_hd_tx guard, Interrupt mode):

-- Register state after spi_write() --
  CR1=0xC040  SR=0x0000
  BIDIOE=1  SPE=1  BSY=0
[CHECK] SPE
  Expected: 1 (waiting for Master clock)
  Actual:   1
-- Master RX attempt --
[CTRL] Master RX: 0x11 (expected 0x11)
>>> TEST 5 PASSED <<<

Full test results - before patch (Interrupt mode):

Test 1: Manual Baseline RX             [PASS]   <- Hardware works
Test 2: Zephyr Slave RX 1byte          [PASS]   <- Driver RX works
Test 3: Manual Slave TX                [PASS]   <- Hardware TX works
Test 4: Zephyr Slave RX 4byte          [FAIL]   <- Separate issue
Test 5: Zephyr Slave TX 1byte          [FAIL]   <- SPE=0 before Master clock
Test 6: Zephyr Slave TX 4byte          [FAIL]   <- Blocks forever (3s timeout)

Full test results - after patch (Interrupt mode):

Test 1: Manual Baseline RX             [PASS]
Test 2: Zephyr Slave RX 1byte          [PASS]
Test 3: Manual Slave TX                [PASS]
Test 4: Zephyr Slave RX 4byte          [FAIL]   <- Separate issue (unchanged)
Test 5: Zephyr Slave TX 1byte          [PASS]   <- Fixed by patch
Test 6: Zephyr Slave TX 4byte          [FAIL]   <- Multi-byte (separate limitation)
```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

- **OS**: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS
- **Toolchain**: Zephyr SDK 0.17.4
- **Zephyr version**: main branch, commit [`96b0b9079eb8`](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/commit/96b0b9079eb881436b68ab4840b5f970284023df) (v4.3.0-5260-g96b0b9079eb)
- **west**: v1.5.0
- **Board**: NUCLEO-F439ZI (STM32F439ZI)
- **SPI driver file**: `drivers/spi/spi_stm32.c`
- **Configs tested**: `CONFIG_SPI_STM32_INTERRUPT=y` and `CONFIG_SPI_STM32_DMA=y`

### Additional Context

Half-duplex support was introduced in #75983, which covers the Master-side paths. This issue addresses the remaining Slave TX case.

A minimal patch (attached: `spi_stm32_fix_slave_hd_tx.patch`) has been verified on hardware - fixes 1-byte Slave Half-Duplex TX (Test 5: FAIL -> PASS). The fix follows the existing `SPI_HOLD_ON_CS` pattern: the application calls `spi_re

[spi_stm32_fix_slave_hd_tx.patch](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25080556/spi_stm32_fix_slave_hd_tx.patch)

lease()` after the Master has clocked out the data.

```shell
407FE000: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 |........ ........|
407FE010: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 |........ ........|
407FE020: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 |........ ........|
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103594.body:
### Describe the bug

Since commit 034220c08ec5dfc871c31f1233aea64e287a922d, the function `bt_hci_stm32wba_close` calls `bt_hci_cmd_alloc` and `bt_hci_cmd_send_sync`, which are not available when `CONFIG_BT_HCI_RAW=y`, i.e. builds without host.

For example, you have a linker error when you compile the sample `hci_uart`.

A possible fix is to guard the offending calls with a check CONFIG_BT_HCI_HOST or directly call the link layer function which perform ACI_RESET.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103615.body:
### Summary

TMAG5170 datasheet specifies a fixed 32-bit SPI frame (32 SCK pulses while CS is held low). The TMAG5170 driver is implemented as a single 4-byte (`uint8_t[4]`) transceive, which already satisfies the 32-bit frame requirement on the wire.

On STM32 targets, configuring `SPI_WORD_SET(32)` results in correct bus activity but incorrect semantics (likely byte order/packing), while `SPI_WORD_SET(8)` works reliably.

Related attempt: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/97327

---

### Repro / Observation

- `SPI_WORD_SET(8)` → driver works, correct values
- `SPI_WORD_SET(32)` → transfers happen and data arrives, but appears byte-swapped/packed differently, so the driver interprets it incorrectly

---

### Why `SPI_WORD_SET(8)` is correct/portable

The driver uses byte-framed buffers:
```c
uint8_t buffer_tx[4] = { BIT(7) | reg, 0x00U, 0x00U, (cmd & 0x0F) << 4 };
uint8_t buffer_rx[4] = { 0 };
spi_transceive_dt(&config->bus, &tx, &rx);
```

It decodes response bytes by fixed indexes:
```c
*output = (buffer_rx[1] << 8) | buffer_rx[2];
```

CRC nibble placement assumes the 4th byte is the last byte on the wire:
```c
#define TMAG5170_SET_CRC(buf, crc) ((uint8_t *)(buf))[3] |= (crc & 0x0F)
#define TMAG5170_ZERO_CRC(buf) ((uint8_t *)(buf))[3] &= 0xF0
```

With `SPI_WORD_SET(32)`, the effective byte order of that 4-byte frame becomes controller-driver dependent (packing/endianness/chunking), which can vary across SoCs. Using `SPI_WORD_SET(8)` keeps the wire byte sequence aligned with the driver’s `uint8_t[4]` layout by construction.

Note: the datasheet’s “32-bit frame” requirement is satisfied by 4×8-bit transfers as long as CS stays asserted for 32 SCK pulses.

---

### Proposal

- Clarify in driver docs/Kconfig help that “32-bit frame” refers to 32 clocks under CS, not 32-bit SPI word size
- Default/recommend `SPI_WORD_SET(8)` for TMAG5170 to avoid controller-dependent packing surprises
- If 32-bit word size must be supported, driver would need explicit pack/unpack with defined endianness, but portability may still be limited

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable.

### Environment

- Zephyr commit: 8238dd80
- Boards: nucleo_u5a5zj_q, nucleo_n657x0_q
- Bus: SPI1 @ 5 MHz
- Sensor: TI TMAG5170 (4-wire SPI)

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103615.title: drivers: sensor: tmag5170 — “fixed 32-bit SPI frame” is not `SPI_WORD_SET(32)`; on STM32U5/N657 `SPI_WORD_SET(32)` causes byte packing/byte order issues, `SPI_WORD_SET(8)` works

### Describe the bug

get_node("/interrupt-map-bitops-test/node@70000000e") should be equal to get_node("/interrupt-map-bitops-test/node@70000000E") this bug created the issue https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/issues/103638

Looking at dtlib is seams that is also does not consider this case from the spec https://devicetree-specification.readthedocs.io/en/latest/chapter2-devicetree-basics.html#path-names as address can also be omitted if path is unambiguous

e.g.

/ {
foo@10 {};
}

both paths -> /foo and /foo@10 should work. looking at scripts/dts/python-devicetree/src/devicetree/dtlib.py the fill name in full with address is always used and this is not correct.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103955.body:
### Summary

Of the 512kB of onboard SRAM, only 425kB is actually usable by applications. This appears to be limited by a calculation made in `soc/espressif/esp32c6/memory.h` to avoid colliding with the bootloader. This makes sense since the bootloader apparently preloads certain RAM segments before launching the application. But once this is done, and the control handed over to the application, the majority of this RAM should be reclaimable. Specifically, the Shared buffers and PRO CPU stack mentioned in memory.h.

### Describe the solution you'd like

As of https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/99309, `DRAM_STACK_START` is used to initialize the CPU stack pointer. If `z_main_stack` was used instead like on other platforms, this area would be completely unused. More importantly, the Shared Buffer region accounts for 70kb and even just reclaiming the buffer region would be a major win.

Even with my admittedly limited understanding of linker scripting, and correct me if I'm wrong, but I feel like it should be possible to include these regions either as uninitialized RAM or as RAM that is only zeroed after exiting the bootloader. Maybe put the initialized RAM segments first, limiting them to `DRAM_BUFFERS_START`, and then non-initialized RAM segments follow but are only limited to within `DRAM_ROM_BSS_DATA_START`?

### Alternatives

We are still working on optimizing our application, but we are early in the prototyping phase and are more worried about getting it working before squeezing our RAM for every byte. Right now, with the WiFi, networking, and bluetooth stacks enabled plus all of our application code we only have a couple dozen KB of headroom which is making some of us nervous.

### Additional Context

Zephyr v4.3.99 ddd25e81e77774bb7bb84f8bd7f8f712e9f9a4b2
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103955.title: ESP32C6 Increase Usable RAM

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103962.body:
### Describe the bug

The `flags` fields of the "Submission Queue Event" is not cleared after claiming the event in the following functions:
* `i2c_rtio_configure`
* `i2c_rtio_configure`
* `i3c_rtio_configure`
* `i3c_rtio_ccc`
* `i3c_rtio_configure`

If the claimed event was previously used for a submission that set `RTIO_SQE_TRANSACTION` or `RTIO_SQE_CHAINED`, these functions would behave incorrectly.

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

- Zephyr v4.3

### Additional Context

Fixed by #100419
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103962.title: RTIO: SQE flags not zeroed by some functions

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103971.title: drivers: pcf8563: Wrong year offset when setting time.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103974.body:
### Describe the bug

I am seeing a crash with ARM based boards when I run west twister -p nrf52840dk/nrf52840 \
  -T zephyr/tests/benchmarks/app_kernel \
  -s benchmark.kernel.application.user \
  --device-testing --device-serial /dev/ttyACM0


The issue is because the pipe directly tires to access userspace memory directly from kernel, and it is leading to MPU_FAULT.

Since this crash occurs on all ARM platforms, is it possible to disable this test for ARM platforms, so that CI/CD never checks for this?

This is tested on multiple ARM based platforms not just nordic.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Steps to reproduce:
Build: `west build -p -b nrf52840dk/nrf52840 zephyr/tests/benchmarks/app_kernel -- -DCONF_FILE=prj_user.conf`
or run the same using twister: `west twister -p nrf52840dk/nrf52840 \
  -T zephyr/tests/benchmarks/app_kernel \
  -s benchmark.kernel.application.user \
  --device-testing --device-serial /dev/ttyACM0`

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103980.body:
This issue was created automatically because the backport of #103762 to `v4.2-branch` failed.

The backport to `v4.2-branch` failed:
```
CONFLICT (modify/delete): drivers/ethernet/eth_stm32_hal_v2.c deleted in HEAD and modified in 8c6670cbd02 (drivers: ethernet: stm32: Set MDIO clock range after MAC reset).  Version 8c6670cbd02 (drivers: ethernet: stm32: Set MDIO clock range after MAC reset) of drivers/ethernet/eth_stm32_hal_v2.c left in tree.
error: could not apply 8c6670cbd02... drivers: ethernet: stm32: Set MDIO clock range after MAC reset
hint: After resolving the conflicts, mark them with
hint: "git add/rm <pathspec>", then run
hint: "git cherry-pick --continue".
hint: You can instead skip this commit with "git cherry-pick --skip".
hint: To abort and get back to the state before "git cherry-pick",
hint: run "git cherry-pick --abort".
hint: Disable this message with "git config set advice.mergeConflict false"
```
To backport manually, run these commands in your terminal:
```bash
# Fetch latest updates from GitHub
git fetch
# Create a new working tree
git worktree add .worktrees/backport-v4.2-branch v4.2-branch
# Navigate to the new working tree
cd .worktrees/backport-v4.2-branch
# Create a new branch
git switch --create backport-103762-to-v4.2-branch
# Cherry-pick the merged commits of this pull request and resolve the conflicts
git cherry-pick -x 8c6670cbd02febf27d85463914cbf3fb26634f81
# Push it to GitHub
git push --set-upstream origin backport-103762-to-v4.2-branch
# Go back to the original working tree
cd ../..
# Delete the working tree
git worktree remove .worktrees/backport-v4.2-branch
```
Then, create a pull request where the `base` branch is `v4.2-branch` and the `compare`/`head` branch is `backport-103762-to-v4.2-branch`.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103992.body:
## Describe the bug
Building for i.MX943 M33 can fail at link time with undefined references from `soc/nxp/imx/imx9/imx943/pm_mcore.c` to NXP SCMI CPU-domain helper APIs.

Example failing symbols (from `pm_state_before()/pm_state_set()/pm_state_exit_post_ops()`):
- `scmi_nxp_cpu_pd_lpm_set`
- `scmi_nxp_cpu_set_irq_mask`
- `scmi_nxp_cpu_sleep_mode_set`

The helpers are implemented in `drivers/firmware/scmi/nxp/cpu.c`, but that file is only built when `CONFIG_NXP_SCMI_CPU_DOMAIN_HELPERS=y` (which itself depends on `CONFIG_ARM_SCMI_NXP_VENDOR_EXTENSIONS` and `DT_HAS_NXP_SCMI_CPU_ENABLED`).

However `pm_mcore.c` is compiled when `CONFIG_SOC_IMX943_PM_MCORE=y` (default y when `PM` is enabled for i.MX943 M33/M7), even if the SCMI CPU helpers are not enabled. This leads to the undefined reference link failures.

Reference line in upstream:
https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/0bb9a469c1dbe1dfb825cd68a08fab8b70c3701e/soc/nxp/imx/imx9/imx943/pm_mcore.c#L139

## To Reproduce
Observed in CI/twister build:
- Test: `cpp.main.newlib.cxx_tests.new_delete`
- Zephyr version: `v4.3.0-5931-g8100484e5d43`
- Platform: `nxp_virtual/mimx94398/m33`

Linker error excerpt:
```
ld.bfd: zephyr/libzephyr.a(pm_mcore.c.obj): in function `pm_state_before':
.../soc/nxp/imx/imx9/imx943/pm_mcore.c:208: undefined reference to `scmi_nxp_cpu_pd_lpm_set'
.../pm_mcore.c:229: undefined reference to `scmi_nxp_cpu_set_irq_mask'
.../pm_mcore.c:280: undefined reference to `scmi_nxp_cpu_sleep_mode_set'
.../pm_mcore.c:304: undefined reference to `scmi_nxp_cpu_sleep_mode_set'
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
```

## Expected behavior
Either:
1. `pm_mcore.c` is not built unless the required SCMI CPU helpers are enabled, **or**
2. `pm_mcore.c` is guarded so it does not reference these symbols when the helpers are not present.

## Proposed fix
In `soc/nxp/imx/imx9/imx943/Kconfig`, make `SOC_IMX943_PM_MCORE` depend on `NXP_SCMI_CPU_DOMAIN_HELPERS`.

Example:
```Kconfig
config SOC_IMX943_PM_MCORE
	bool "i.MX943 multi-core PM shared logic"
	default y
	depends on PM
	depends on SOC_MIMX94398_M33 || SOC_MIMX94398_M7_0 || SOC_MIMX94398_M7_1
	depends on NXP_SCMI_CPU_DOMAIN_HELPERS
```

This ensures the SoC PM implementation is only built when the implementation of `scmi_nxp_cpu_*` helpers is available.

## Additional context
- Definitions are in: `drivers/firmware/scmi/nxp/cpu.c`
- Built under: `CONFIG_NXP_SCMI_CPU_DOMAIN_HELPERS` (`drivers/firmware/scmi/nxp/CMakeLists.txt`)

## Environment
- Toolchain: zephyr-sdk 0.17.1-rc4 (arm-zephyr-eabi gcc 12.2.0)
- Host: Linux (Jenkins CI)
- version: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/commit/2fd6d9ad8d36918d98fd2747a9b4fb8f3c07c971
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103992.title: imx943: pm_mcore.c causes link failure when NXP SCMI CPU domain helpers are not enabled (undefined scmi_nxp_cpu_* references)

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103997.body:
### Describe the bug

this is found in customized board testing we can name it virtual_board

`tests/drivers/i2s/i2s_speed` fails to build for `virtual_board/mimxrt1189/cm33` because the MCUX SAI I2S driver enforces `CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE > 3`, but the MCUX eDMA Kconfig default is `2`.

The failure occurs at compile time in `drivers/i2s/i2s_mcux_sai.c`:

```c
#define NUM_DMA_BLOCKS_RX_PREP 3
#define MAX_TX_DMA_BLOCKS CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE
BUILD_ASSERT(MAX_TX_DMA_BLOCKS > NUM_DMA_BLOCKS_RX_PREP,
            "NUM_DMA_BLOCKS_RX_PREP must be < CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE");
```

### To reproduce
1. Build the test:
   - Test: `drivers.i2s.speed.drivers_i2s_speed_both_rxtx.i2s_dir_both_transfer_long_44100`
   - Platform: `mimxrt1189/cm33`
2. Observe build failure in `drivers/i2s/i2s_mcux_sai.c`.

### Build log excerpt
```
.../zephyr/include/zephyr/toolchain/gcc.h:87:36: error: static assertion failed: "NUM_DMA_BLOCKS_RX_PREP must be < CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE"
.../zephyr/drivers/i2s/i2s_mcux_sai.c:44:1: note: in expansion of macro 'BUILD_ASSERT'
```

### Environment
- Zephyr: `v4.3.0-5938-gd5f09c1b0464`
- Toolchain: GCC (as used by CI)

### Expected behavior
The `i2s_speed` test should build on `nxp_virtual/mimxrt1189/cm33` without requiring manual Kconfig overrides.

### Proposed fix
Update the test configuration to ensure an adequate TCD queue size when using MCUX eDMA + MCUX SAI I2S, e.g.:

- Add to `tests/drivers/i2s/i2s_speed/prj.conf` (simple but may cause “undefined symbol” warnings on non-MCUX platforms):
  ```conf
  CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE=4
  ```

or (preferred to avoid affecting other platforms)

- Add a board-specific test config file for the affected platform (e.g. `tests/drivers/i2s/i2s_speed/boards/nxp_virtual_mimxrt1189_cm33.conf`) containing:
  ```conf
  CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE=4
  ```

### version:

os: ubuntu
toolchain: zephyr
version: 2fd6d9ad8d36918d98fd2747a9b4fb8f3c07c971 

### Additional context
`drivers/dma/Kconfig.mcux_edma` currently defaults `CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE` to `2`, which is incompatible with the current constraints in `drivers/i2s/i2s_mcux_sai.c` for this test scenario.
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#103997.title: tests/drivers/i2s/i2s_speed: mimxrt1189/cm33: build fails due to CONFIG_DMA_TCD_QUEUE_SIZE too small for i2s_mcux_sai

### Steps to reproduce

To reproduce, follow these instructions that attempt to build the `net/openthread/shell` sample. Option 1 is the "control", it works as expected. Option 2 is a minimal project reproducing the issue I'm facing.

**Option 1**

```shell
mkdir zephyr-west-yml
cd zephyr-west-yml
west init
west update
```

```shell
cd zephyr
west build -p always -b nrf52840dk/nrf52840 samples/net/openthread/shell
```

**Option 2** 
1. Create the project structure:
```
mkdir -p project-west-yml/project
cd project-west-yml/project
```
2. Create a `west.yml` file containing:
```
manifest:
  remotes:
    - name: zephyrproject-rtos
      url-base: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos
  projects:
    - name: zephyr
      remote: zephyrproject-rtos
      revision: main
      import:
        path-prefix: deps
        name-allowlist:
          - cmsis_6
          - hal_nordic
          - littlefs
          - mbedtls
          - mcuboot
          - openthread
          - zcbor
```
3. Fetch Zephyr and the necessary modules:
```
cd ..
west init -l project
west update
```
4. Build the `net/openthread/shell` sample. It fails.
```
cd deps/zephyr
west build -p always -b nrf52840dk/nrf52840 samples/net/openthread/shell
```

### Impact

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104200.body:
### Summary

I just stumbled over the fact that `WRITE_BIT` in the `util_macro.h` does not work for bit positions >=32 for 64bit bitmasks because `BIT` uses `1UL`. There is already `BIT64` defined using `1ULL` but there is no `WRITE_BIT64` macro.

Well, for now I added it into my application, but I felt better if I could just use it from the header.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Suggestion:
- add a hint that one could run into that problem
- add the `WRITE_BIT64` macro

```c

/**
 * @brief Set or clear a bit depending on a boolean value in an unsigned integer
 *
 * The argument @p var is a variable whose value is written to as a
 * side effect.
 * 
 * For 64 bit unsigned integers consider using `WRITE_BIT64`.
 *
 * @param var Variable to be altered
 * @param bit Bit number
 * @param set if 0, clears @p bit in @p var; any other value sets @p bit
 */
#define WRITE_BIT(var, bit, set) \
	((var) = (set) ? ((var) | BIT(bit)) : ((var) & ~BIT(bit)))


/**
 * @brief Set or clear a bit depending on a boolean value in a 64 bit unsigned integer
 *
 * The argument @p var is a variable whose value is written to as a
 * side effect.
 *
 * @param var Variable to be altered
 * @param bit Bit number
 * @param set if 0, clears @p bit in @p var; any other value sets @p bit
 */
#define WRITE_BIT64(var, bit, set) \
	((var) = (set) ? ((var) | BIT64(bit)) : ((var) & ~BIT64(bit)))
```

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104200.title: add `WRITE_BIT64` into `zephyr/sys/util_macro.h`

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104210.body:
### Describe the bug

This behaviour was observed when using Modbus Subsys in Slave mode with `CONFIG_UART_INTERRUPT_DRIVEN` on MAX32670. Modbus slave works by waiting for a command over RX with TX initially disabled. Upon successful command, RX is disabled and TX is enabled with the transmission array being daisy chained through TX interrupts. The final TX interrupt checks that it is safe to disable TX by calling `uart_irq_tx_complete`. Normally, this will then disable TX and enable RX, however, this check fails causing MODBUS to never be able to receive subsequent commands. See https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/main/subsys/modbus/modbus_serial.c#L424C6-L424C26

The cause of this bug seems to be timing related. This issue does not occur if I set a break point at the `uart_irq_tx_complete` and wait momentarily before continuing. The reason for this in my opinion is the `MXC_F_UART_REVB_STATUS_TX_BUSY` bit likely hasn’t changed yet when in the IRQ (i.e. the TX isn't actually complete). Looking at the MAX32670 User Guide, the TX interrupt occurs when the TX FIFO is almost empty (i.e. "there is one byte remaining in the transmit FIFO") so it's possible we haven't actually finished transmitting by the time we reach this check, leading to the bug.

This bug is quite important as it could potentially also break other SubSys' that rely on `uart_irq_tx_complete`. I'm not sure what the ideal fix would be here - ideally we don't want to add any delays or polling, however, `uart_irq_tx_complete` should give reliable information of the TX state so we can safely shutdown TX in an IRQ context. 

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

I encountered this issue when running my own modbus App with 115200 baud on MAX32670, however, I have seen issue should occur when running the modbus `rtu_server` sample. You should be able to replicate as follows:
1. Flash `rtu_server` with CONFIG_UART_INTERRUPT_DRIVEN to max32670evkit.
2. Send a modbus command over UART and observe a response packet.
3. Sent a modbus command over UART and observe no response packet.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104213.body:
### Summary

Max32 PWM driver is currently static as the user can only set the cycles using a generic method. Adding support for PWM events would allow for more flexibility and control over PWM as the PERIOD callback could allow for more dynamic use cases such as updating the frequency at the end of a set number of periods. See this [sample](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/main/samples/drivers/pwm/event/src/main.c).

### Describe the solution you'd like

MAX32 MCUs have native support within their timers to enable a software interrupt at the end of a PWM timer cycle so hardware support should be there. Support should be straightforward. All that is needed is an ISR which calls pwm_fire_event_callbacks with the event that caused the interrupt. Additionally, manage_event_callback must also be implemented that dynamically enables/disables the ISR depending on the added event type.

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104213.title: driver: pwm: pwm_max32: Add support for PWM Events

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104214.body:
### Summary

Max32 SPI driver will currently use interrupt-driven approach for ASYNC transactions instead of DMA when CONFIG_SPI_MAX32_DMA is defined and the device has DMA channels assigned. We should ideally use DMA for this if it's available.

### Describe the solution you'd like

There are other improvements I'd like to see in the SPI DMA driver, however, I suggest simply adding baseline support before improving the implementation overall. My suggestion is to build of the current SYNC implementation for the initial transfer, subsequent transfers can then be schedule using `k_work_submit` from the DMA IRQ as done in the ASYNC INTERRUPT approach.

### Alternatives

I have considered directly scheduling the next SPI transfer from the IRQ, however, the time taken for scheduling SPI transactions over DMA is currently around 100us on MAX32670. This is too long and could lead to ISR starvation

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104214.title: driver: spi: spi_max32: Add DMA support for transceive_async

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104253.body:
### Describe the bug

Currently STM32 LSE driving capability is set using `RCC_BDCR_LSEDRV_Pos`, however STM32L0 defines `RCC_CSR_LSEDRV_Pos` instead of the used one.

https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/b70f7d28c623ee3e66737441b962de1a4cfda389/drivers/clock_control/clock_stm32_ll_common.c#L1015-L1018

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Set LSE clock driving capability in overlay file using for instance `nucleo_l053r8` board:

```dts
&clk_lse {
	driving-capability = <3>;
	status = "okay";
};
```

### Relevant log output

```shell
FAILED: zephyr/drivers/clock_control/CMakeFiles/drivers__clock_control.dir/clock_stm32_ll_common.c.obj

...

/opt/toolchains/zephyr/drivers/clock_control/clock_stm32_ll_common.c: In function 'set_up_fixed_clock_sources':
/opt/toolchains/zephyr/drivers/clock_control/clock_stm32_ll_common.c:677:68: error: 'RCC_BDCR_LSEDRV_Pos' undeclared (first use in this function); did you mean 'RCC_CSR_LSEDRV_Pos'?
  677 |                 LL_RCC_LSE_SetDriveCapability(STM32_LSE_DRIVING << RCC_BDCR_LSEDRV_Pos);
      |                                                                    ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
      |                                                                    RCC_CSR_LSEDRV_Pos
```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

Linux
Zephyr v3.7.1 (but looking at sousces main branch is buggy too)
SDK v0.16.9
App `samples/drivers/uart/echo_bot` using board `nucleo_l053r8`

### Additional Context

An app-side workaround is adding to `CMakeLists.txt`:

```cmake
zephyr_compile_definitions(RCC_BDCR_LSEDRV_Pos=RCC_CSR_LSEDRV_Pos)
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104273.body:
### Describe the bug

The `bt_cap_commander_cancel` and `bt_cap_initiator_unicast_audio_cancel` function are intended to immediately abort a procedure, typically triggered by a timeout or some error. 

The implementations of these are however not thread safe, and if done in a separate thread than the BT RX thread, they may cause fatal memory issues. E.g. in 

```c
		proc_param = get_next_proc_param(active_proc);
		__ASSERT(proc_param != NULL, "proc is not done, but could not get next proc_param");
		next_cap_stream = proc_param->stream;
		next_bap_stream = &next_cap_stream->bap_stream;

		active_proc->proc_initiated_cnt++;

		proc_param->in_progress = true;
		codec_cfg = proc_param->start.codec_cfg;
```

`proc_param` may be non-null, but is then cleared alongside all other memory in `active_proc` when `bt_cap_initiator_unicast_audio_cancel` is called. 

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

No explicit steps to reproduce, but calling e.g. `bt_cap_initiator_unicast_audio_cancel` at random times from a non-BT RX thread should trigger this issue. 

### Relevant log output

```shell
N/A
```

### Impact

Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104273.title: Bluetooth: CAP: Cancel functions may cause fatal errors

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104286.body:
### Describe the bug

`net_if_ipv6_router_add` causes the new router entry's `is_default` value to be set to `lifetime != 0`. If `is_default` isn't set to true, I don't think it is ever used for routing.

`net_if_ipv6_router_add` calls `iface_router_add` with `is_default` set to false, but that doesn't matter since `iface_router_add` doesn't care about it anyway for IPv6. It sets `is_default` to true or false depending on if `lifetime is != 0` or not. For IPv4 it then overwrites it with the is_default argument.

Maybe it should be like that, but then it seems it would be better to give an error and also documented it (well, what 0 means should be documented anyway). The system probably shouldn't send Neighbor Solicitation messages to addresses outside of the interface's network when there is no default route either.


A workaround is probably (have not tested it yet) to not use an infinite lifetime and instead periodically call net_if_ipv6_router_update_lifetime.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Add the following to the hello world sample.

prj.conf:
```
CONFIG_POSIX_API=y
CONFIG_NET_SOCKETS=y
CONFIG_NETWORKING=y
CONFIG_ETH_NATIVE_TAP=y
CONFIG_NET_IPV6=y
CONFIG_NET_UDP=y
CONFIG_NET_TCP=y
CONFIG_NET_SOCKETS_PACKET=y
CONFIG_NET_CONFIG_SETTINGS=y
CONFIG_NET_CONFIG_AUTO_INIT=y
CONFIG_NET_CONFIG_INIT_TIMEOUT=5

CONFIG_NET_LOG=y
CONFIG_NET_TCP_LOG_LEVEL_DBG=y
CONFIG_NET_IF_LOG_LEVEL_DBG=y
CONFIG_NET_IPV6_LOG_LEVEL_DBG=y
```

Interface setup code:
```
   int ipv6_ns = 64;
   char *ipv6_addr = "2001:fd8::20";
   char *ipv6_gw = "2001:fd8::10";
   net_config_init_app(NULL, "Init");
    struct net_if *iface = net_if_get_default();
    struct in6_addr ipv6_addr_struct;
    net_addr_pton(AF_INET6, ipv6_addr, &ipv6_addr_struct);
    net_if_ipv6_addr_add(iface, &ipv6_addr_struct, NET_ADDR_MANUAL, 0);
    net_if_ipv6_prefix_add(iface, &ipv6_addr_struct, ipv6_ns, 0);
    struct in6_addr ipv6_gw_struct;
    net_addr_pton(AF_INET6, ipv6_gw, &ipv6_gw_struct);
    net_if_ipv6_router_add(iface, &ipv6_gw_struct, 0);
```

zeth.conf:
```
INTERFACE="$1"

HWADDR="00:00:5e:00:53:10"

IPV6_ADDR_1="2001:db8::10"
IPV6_ROUTE_1="2001:db8::/64"

IPV4_ADDR_1="192.168.10.10/24"
IPV4_ROUTE_1="192.168.10.0/24"

ip link set dev $INTERFACE up

ip link set dev $INTERFACE address $HWADDR

ip -6 address add $IPV6_ADDR_1 dev $INTERFACE nodad
ip -6 route add $IPV6_ROUTE_1 dev $INTERFACE
ip address add $IPV4_ADDR_1 dev $INTERFACE
ip route add $IPV4_ROUTE_1 dev $INTERFACE > /dev/null 2>&1
```

Build for the native_sim board.

Setup the networking with tools/net-tools/net-setup.sh start --config zeth.conf --iface zeth

Start the firmware with "-eth-if=zeth" as argument.

Turn on IPv6 forwarding on the host:
`sudo sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=1`

Then ping the board:
`ping 2001:db8::20`
That should work.

ping -I 2001:db9::10 2001:db8::20
That does not work, but should.

2001:db9::10 must of course have been configured on another interface. Its easiest to copy the zeth.conf file above and create another tun/tap interface. It's source address can be used with ping even without a firmware using the interface.


### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

Linux with Zephyr SDK.
v4.3.0 of Zephyr.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104300.body:
### Describe the bug

When compiling any application with RAIL enabled (`CONFIG_SOC_GECKO_USE_RAIL=y`) for a `efr32fg1p` device, it fails.
The reason being, that `zephyr/modules/hal_silabs/gecko/CMakeLists.txt` looks for a binary blob named `librail_efr32xg1p_gcc_release.a`, while the real name of the fetched blob is `librail_efr32xg1_gcc_release.a` (no `p`).
Same thing with `librail_multiprotocol_efr32xg1p_gcc_release.a`.

My idea on how to fix the problem it to either rename the blobs to include a `p`, or fix this:
```cmake
# Get SoC series number, i.e. translate e.g. efr32bg22 -> 22
string(SUBSTRING ${CONFIG_SOC_SERIES} 7 2 GECKO_SERIES_NUMBER)
```
to not always take two charactes, but rather take **up to** two **numeric-only** characters.

In addition, there's also this included directory in the same `CMakeLists.txt` file, which doesn't seem to affect building but is clearly also problematic:
```cmake
${RADIO_DIR}/rail_lib/plugin/rail_util_protocol/config/efr32xg${GECKO_SERIES_NUMBER}/
```
The actual directory is named `efr32xg1**x**`! `1p` wouldn't work, and so wouldn't `11`, `12` or others.
I'm not sure on the correct approach to fix here because I don't get what silabs intended with this include.

Thanks!

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Fetch hal_silabs blobs: `west blobs fetch hal_silabs`
Add `CONFIG_SOC_GECKO_USE_RAIL=y` to `zephyr/samples/hello_world/prj.conf`
Run `west build --board slwrb4250b zephyr/samples/hello_world`

### Relevant log output

```shell
(.venv) user@USER-PC:~/zephyrproject$ west build --board slwrb4250b zephyr/samples/hello_world
ninja: error: '/home/user/zephyrproject/modules/hal/silabs/zephyr/blobs/gecko/platform/radio/rail_lib/autogen/librail_release/librail_efr32xg1p_gcc_release.a', needed by 'zephyr/zephyr_pre0.elf', missing and no known rule to make it
FATAL ERROR: command exited with status 1: /usr/bin/cmake --build /home/user/zephyrproject/build
```

### Impact

Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable.

### Environment

Zephyr 4.3.0

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104350.body:
### Summary

Basic power management support was added to the first in-tree CAN controller driver with https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/96039 (and subsequent PRs https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/104080 and https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/104182).

As further additions of power management support to in-tree CAN controller drivers are being made (https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/100520 and https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/104218), now is suitable time to establish a policy for uniform behaviour across both CAN controller and CAN transceiver drivers.

The main goals of implementing power management support in CAN controllers, at least so far, are:
* Power saving.
* Remote wake-up on CAN activity.

### Describe the solution you'd like

#### Initial Steps

- [x] Review and merge https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/104182 to create a stable base for testing the current TCAN4x5x driver behaviour.
- [x] Establish a test suite for the currently implemented power management behaviour. This test suite will serve as a baseline for future PM additions and potential changes in driver behaviour (https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/104369)

#### Initial CAN Controller Driver Behaviour

The behaviour below was established in https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/pull/96039 but is subject to change as power management support is added to more CAN controller drivers:

* The device is marked busy while started.
* The device is marked busy while RX filters are added (this is due to a limitation on the TCAN4x5x where RX filters and all other configuration are lost in sleep mode).
* The device is marked non-busy when stopped and no RX filters are added.
* CAN controller timing settings are lost on suspend.
* CAN controller mode settings are lost on suspend.

#### Unresolved Behaviour

- [ ] Is it common for CAN controllers to lose RX filter configuration in sleep mode? If not, the TCAN4x5x driver should be modified to restore the RX filters on resume and the test suite should be changed to verify that RX filters are preserved through suspend.
- [ ] Should CAN controller timing settings be restored on resume? Likely yes.
- [ ] Should CAN controller mode be restored on resume? Likely yes.
- [ ] Should calling a CAN controller driver API on a suspended CAN controller device automatically resume the device? If not, the call should fail. Currently, the behaviour of calling a CAN controller API on a suspended device is undefined.
- [ ] Client code currently has no way of being notified when a CAN controller is suspended. The `enum can_state` should be expanded with a `CAN_STATE_SUSPENDED` state and the state callback called upon suspension. Should there be a separate state representing `PM_DEVICE_STATE_OFF`?
- [ ] Remote wake-up configuration (i.e. wake-up on CAN activity) should adhere to the `wakeup-source` devicetree property and `pm_device_wakeup_enable()`.
- [ ] CAN transceiver drivers (currently only one present in-tree) should handle PM on their own, but should suspending a CAN controller automatically suspend the associated transceiver? Likely ~~yes~~ no.

  Currently, we only have a simple GPIO-controlled CAN transceiver driver in-tree, but the plan is to add support for CAN Partial Networking (CAN PN), where the CAN transceiver is more "intelligent" and can handle waking up the CAN controller when a pre-configured CAN frame/pattern is received on the CAN bus. If the CAN controller was to unconditionally suspend the CAN transceiver when going into suspend itself, that would complicate this future addition.
- [ ] Should/can behaviour differ between `PM_DEVICE_STATE_SUSPENDED` and `PM_DEVICE_STATE_OFF`? Should the TCAN4x5x driver really be entering "stand-by mode" on `PM_DEVICE_ACTION_SUSPEND` and "sleep mode" on `PM_DEVICE_ACTION_TURN_OFF`?

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

Feel free to comment on this issue and/or join the discussion thread on Discord: https://discord.com/channels/720317445772017664/1474400413951459511

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104362.body:
### Describe the bug

The nsim/nsim_em7d_v22 board fails userspace memory domain tests because it only has 3 MPU regions available for memory domain partitions, but the tests require 4.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Test 1: shared_mem sample
```bash
west build -b nsim/nsim_em7d_v22 samples/userspace/shared_mem -T sample.kernel.memory_protection.shared_mem -p
west flash
```

Test 2: userspace test
```bash
west build -b nsim/nsim_em7d_v22 tests/kernel/mem_protect/userspace -T kernel.memory_protection.userspace -p
west flash
```

### Relevant log output

Test 1 (shared_mem):
```shell
E: num_parts of 4 exceeds maximum allowable partitions (3)
ASSERTION FAIL [ret == 0] @ main.c:144
    k_mem_domain_init() on enc_domain failed -22
```

Test 2 (userspace):
```shell
E: no free partition slots available
Assertion failed: (k_mem_domain_add_partition(&k_mem_domain_default, &alt_part) not equal to 0)
failed to add memory partition
```

### Impact

Annoyance – Minor irritation; no significant impact on usability or functionality.

### Environment

OS: Linux
Toolchain: Zephyr SDK
Commit SHA or Version used: Main a6fb8b8a19f9052bd21590e3fab03b2d2cff7cb1

### Additional Context

**Root Cause**
The `nsim_em7d_v22` board uses ARC MPU v2 with limited hardware MPU regions. After allocating regions for:
- Static regions (ICCM, DCCM, PERIPHERAL)
- Thread stack regions
- Only 3 regions remain for memory domain partitions. However:
shared_mem sample needs 4 partitions (z_libc_partition, enc_part, red_part, blk_part)
userspace test dynamically adds partitions and runs out of space
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104362.title: nsim/nsim_em7d_v22: userspace memory domain tests fail due to limited MPU partition regions

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104389.body:
### Summary

`originate_call` in `tbs.c` handles the case where we are originating a call. This can either be triggered by a remote control point operation, or a local call to `bt_tbs_originate`.

In the former (remote) call, the implementation looks up the appropriate TBS instance, and performs the action on that TBS instance. 

In the latter (local) call, the implementation performs the operation directly on the provided instance. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

The way that originate works should be the same. 

There are effectively 2 approaches when the operation is done on GTBS:
1) Perform the operation directly on the instance that gets the operation. This implies that GTBS may have calls that are unique to it, and are not part of any TBS instances. (current implementation of `bt_tbs_originate`). 

2) Lookup a TBS instance based on the URI, and forward the operation to a fitting TBS instance based on the URI. This approach uses an implicit choices that the application may not be aware of, and worse if the client only supports GTBS, then the call state notifications will come from an unexpected handle (TBS), and may cause IOP issues. 

Neither approach is really perfect, but 2) does seem more harmful than 1). 

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104389.title: Bluetooth: TBS: `originate_call` inconsistent behavior for GTBS

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104435.body:
### Describe the bug

When building PWM applications with `CONFIG_PWM_CAPTURE` enabled, the build fails with undefined definitions for `PWM_SetFilterSampleCount` and `PWM_SetFilterSamplePeriod`. These function definitions are guarded by `FSL_FEATURE_PWM_HAS_INPUT_FILTER_CAPTURE` flag which is not defined for RT11XX family. The following change fixes the build:
```
--- a/drivers/pwm/pwm_mcux.c
+++ b/drivers/pwm/pwm_mcux.c
@@ -464,11 +464,14 @@ static int mcux_pwm_configure_capture(const struct device *dev,
        /* Setup input capture on channel */
        PWM_SetupInputCapture(config->base, config->index, pwm_channel, &capture_config);
 
+#if defined(FSL_FEATURE_PWM_HAS_INPUT_FILTER_CAPTURE) && \
+       (FSL_FEATURE_PWM_HAS_INPUT_FILTER_CAPTURE == 1U)
        /* Set capture filter */
        PWM_SetFilterSampleCount(config->base, pwm_channel, config->index,
                config->input_filter_count);
        PWM_SetFilterSamplePeriod(config->base, pwm_channel, config->index,
                config->input_filter_period);
+#endif
 
        return 0;
 }

```
but the PWM capture doesn't seem to work for simple loopback test from channel A to B of PWM submodule 0:
```
 <wrn> pwm_capture: pwm capture timed out
```

Have anyone tested FlexPWM driver for input capture (with/without input filter) on RT11XX series?
Is this a known issue?

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Build applications for NXP RT11XX family with `CONFIG_PWM_CAPTURE=y` and `pwm_capture_cycles` API used in code.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104442.body:
### Describe the bug

### **Description**
The Goodix GT9271 touch controller is currently not handled correctly by the Zephyr GT911 driver.

The GT9217 chip reports a different Product ID than the one expected and checked in the Zephyr driver implementation. Because of this, the driver does not properly recognize or initialize the controller.

According to Kconfig.gt911, the driver is intended to support the following Goodix controllers:
"This driver should support gt9110, gt912, gt927, gt9271, gt928"

However, at the moment only the GT911 appears to be supported in the driver code. Other listed controllers (including GT9271) are not properly detected due to strict Product ID checking.

### **Expected Behavior**
The driver should recognize and properly initialize all supported Goodix controllers listed in Kconfig.gt911, or at least handle compatible Product IDs such as the one reported by GT9271.

### **Actual Behavior**
The driver only accepts the GT911 Product ID and fails to work with GT9271.

### **Suggested Fix**
Extend the Product ID check in the driver to include GT9271 (and potentially other compatible Goodix variants listed in Kconfig.gt911).

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

_No response_

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Functional Limitation – Some features not working as expected, but system usable.

### Environment

Zephyr 4.3.0

### Additional Context

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104523.body:
Hi 

I am trying to get a nuccleo board with an STM32F207zg to run with two CAN buses at the same time.
CAN1 works as intended, CAN2 could send frames but would not recieve. 

With some debugging I found that this IF statement would never run for the CAN2 device, when trying to add an RX filter. This implies that can and master_can is the same for CAN2, which causes it to write the filter values to the wrong register.

https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/7c0e0bf99bd5e97c2b6b51bb5b127bab17796220/drivers/can/can_stm32_bxcan.c#L910-L914

Looking further, this macro is responsible for setting that value and it expects a master_can_reg from the devicetree, which seems to be missing. 

https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/7c0e0bf99bd5e97c2b6b51bb5b127bab17796220/drivers/can/can_stm32_bxcan.c#L1142-L1152

Adding this to my project overlay has fixed the issue. 

`&can2 {
    master-can-reg = <0x40006400>;
};`





### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

- Create a new blank project with following .conf 
```
# UART Console
CONFIG_SERIAL=y
CONFIG_CONSOLE=y
CONFIG_UART_CONSOLE=y

# Shell subsystem
CONFIG_SHELL=y
CONFIG_SHELL_BACKEND_SERIAL=y
CONFIG_SHELL_PROMPT_UART="Uart Shell: "

CONFIG_CAN=y
CONFIG_EVENTS=y
CONFIG_CAN_SHELL=y

# Enable debug logging for CAN driver
CONFIG_CAN_LOG_LEVEL_DBG=y
```
- In UART console execute the following commands: 
``` 
can mode can1 loopback
can mode can2 loopback 
can filter add can1 -e 0 0
can filter add can2 -e 0 0 
can start can1 
can start can2 
can send can1 -e 25 1 2 3 4 5
can send can2 -e 25 1 2 3 4 5
``` 
- The RX frame for CAN1 will be printed out, CAN2 will not. 

### Relevant log output

Before applying the fix, CAN debug does not print the RX frame on CAN2. 

```shell 
*** Booting Zephyr OS build 81c77d59d12c ***

Uart Shell: can mode can1 loopback
setting mode 0x00000001
Uart Shell: can mode can2 loopback
setting mode 0x00000001
Uart Shell: can filter add can1 -e 0 0
adding filter with extended (29-bit) CAN ID 0x00000000, CAN ID mask 0x00000000
filter ID: 0
Uart Shell: can filter add can2 -e 0 0
adding filter with extended (29-bit) CAN ID 0x00000000, CAN ID mask 0x00000000
filter ID: 0
Uart Shell: can start can1 
starting can1
Uart Shell: can start can2
starting can2
Uart Shell: can send can1 -e 25 1 2 3 4 5
enqueuing CAN frame #0 with extended (29-bit) CAN ID 0x00000025, RTR 0, CAN FD 0, BRS 0, DLC 5
CAN frame #0 successfully sent
can@40006400  00000025   [5]  01 02 03 04 05 
Uart Shell: can send can2 -e 25 1 2 3 4 5
enqueuing CAN frame #1 with extended (29-bit) CAN ID 0x00000025, RTR 0, CAN FD 0, BRS 0, DLC 5
CAN frame #1 successfully sent
Uart Shell: 
```

After applying the fix in the project overlay, CAN debug print the CAN2 RX frame. 

```shell
*** Booting Zephyr OS build 81c77d59d12c ***

Uart Shell: can mode can1 loopback
setting mode 0x00000001
Uart Shell: can mode can2 loopback
setting mode 0x00000001
Uart Shell: can filter add can1 -e 0 0

starting can2

enqueuing CAN frame #0 with extended (29-bit) CAN ID 0x00000025, RTR 0, CAN FD 0, BRS 0, DLC 5
CAN frame #0 successfully sent
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104530.body:
### Summary

The RISC-V CLIC interrupt controller driver has a race condition when accessing MISELECT/MIREG registers from ISR context. This affects all CLIC driver functions that use the non-memory-mapped register access path. **This issue has been fixed by using the atomic `micsr_*` helpers introduced in the ICSR extension.**

## Problem Description

The CLIC driver uses a two-step sequence to access indirect registers:
1. `csr_write(CSR_MISELECT, register_index)` - Select the target register
2. `csr_read/write/set/clear(CSR_MIREG/MIREG2, value)` - Access the selected register

**Race condition scenario:**
1. ISR calls `riscv_clic_irq_set_pending()` to trigger software interrupts (deferred work)
2. Step 1 executes: `csr_write(CSR_MISELECT, CLIC_INTIP(irq))`
3. Higher priority interrupt preempts before step 2
4. Nested ISR calls any CLIC function, changing MISELECT
5. Original ISR resumes and executes step 2 on wrong register
6. Corruption occurs: wrong register modified

## Impact

- Incorrect interrupt enable/disable states
- Wrong priority settings applied to interrupts
- Spurious or missed software interrupts
- General interrupt controller corruption
- System instability in multi-interrupt scenarios

### Describe the solution you'd like

The atomic `micsr_*` helpers in `include/zephyr/arch/riscv/icsr.h` already provide IRQ-locked access to MISELECT/MIREG registers:

```c
static inline unsigned long micsr_read(unsigned int index)
{
    unsigned int key = irq_lock();
    csr_write(MISELECT, index);
    unsigned long val = csr_read(MIREG);
    irq_unlock(key);
    return val;
}
// ... similar for micsr_write, micsr_set, micsr_clear, etc.
```

The fix updates all CLIC driver functions to use these atomic helpers instead of direct CSR access.

### Alternatives

No alternative is considered other than fixing the potential race condition as described above.

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104530.title: drivers: interrupt_controller: riscv_clic: fix MISELECT/MIREG race condition

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104723.body:
### Describe the bug

It is possible for `random_linkaddr` in the Ethernet bridge to return an address with the multicast bit set. Also, since the address is random, it should always have the LLA bit set. This is already handled correctly in other virtual interfaces.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

Create a bridge, observe that on average, it doesn't work half the time since the randomly generated MAC address has the multicast bit set. Other network devices will drop such packets with the multicast bit set in the source address.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Impact

Annoyance – Minor irritation; no significant impact on usability or functionality.

### Environment

_No response_

### Additional Context

fix:
```c
static void random_linkaddr(uint8_t *linkaddr, size_t len)
{
	sys_rand_get(linkaddr, len);

	linkaddr[0] |= 0x02;  /* force LAA bit */
	linkaddr[0] &= ~0x01; /* clear multicast bit */
}
```

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104751.body:
### Summary

The `bt_iso_chan.required_sec_level` is a minor QoL improvement specific for CIS (as it does not apply to BIS). The `required_sec_level` is not something that is defined by any specification, but was modelled after the l2cap.required_sec_level. 

The security level of CIS follows the ACL, and the security level of BIS depends on the broadcast code. 

The complexity of the implementation of `required_sec_level` for CIS does not match the minor QoL improvement, as the application setting `required_sec_level` to any value can just as easily just call `bt_conn_set_security`. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

Remove the `required_sec_level` for `struct bt_iso_chan` (since it's not a stable API, we do not need to go through the deprecation process), and remove all uses of it:

- iso_chan_connect_security
- bt_iso_security_changed
- BT_ISO_STATE_ENCRYPT_PENDING
- Anything related to CONFIG_BT_SMP in iso.c

While this can be removed without deprecation, the migration guide should be updated to reflect the change and provide instructions on how to set security for CIS. 

### Alternatives

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104751.title: Bluetooth: ISO: Remove required_sec_level

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104757.body:
### Describe the bug

I am quite often using `west build -t menuconfig` to modify some parameters during development. Recently I have observed that this quickly leads to build failures due to dependency cycle on offsets.h. After investigation I have observed that it is not necessary to run menuconfig, just modifying `zephyr/.config` inside build directory is enough.

The issue bisects to 518d8420c9d90e390f8aa29d51443a21f6f44aac

### Regression

- [x] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

`west build -p -b native_sim -d build-usb-test tests/subsys/usb/device_next/ && (cd build-usb-test && touch zephyr/.config && ninja && ninja)`

### Relevant log output

```shell

[tmon@tmon-laptop build-usb-test]$ ninja

-- Generated pickled edt: /home/tmon/zephyrproject/zephyr/build-usb-test/zephyr/edt.pickle
-- Generated devicetree_generated.h: /home/tmon/zephyrproject/zephyr/build-usb-test/zephyr/include/generated/zephyr/devicetree_generated.h
Parsing /home/tmon/zephyrproject/zephyr/Kconfig
Loaded configuration '/home/tmon/zephyrproject/zephyr/build-usb-test/zephyr/.config'

-- Using ccache: /usr/bin/ccache

```

### Impact

Major – Severely degrades functionality; workaround is difficult or unavailable.

### Environment

- Linux
- Zephyr SDK 0.17.4
- Zephyr SHA 7c37e958750a66c2f2706a157f24d24fe5e51591

### Additional Context

Seems to trigger for every target, `tests/subsys/usb/device_next/` is used as example.

Workaround is to append `-- G'Unix Makefiles'` to `west build` command but the build is significantly slower compared to ninja.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104777.body:
### Describe the bug

Using the following sequence leads to a null pointer access:
1. mount FAT fs (success)
2. mount FAT fs (fails)
3. any access of FAT fs

The second mount command overwrites the `mnt_point` string pointer with a null pointer after it fails.

### Regression

- [ ] This is a regression.

### Steps to reproduce

1. `cd samples/subsys/shell/fs`
2. add `CONFIG_FAT_FILESYSTEM_ELM=y` and `CONFIG_FILE_SYSTEM_SHELL_MOUNT_COMMAND=y` to `prj.conf`
3. west build -b nucleo_h563zi
4. west flash
5. uart:~$ fs mount fat /mp0
6. uart:~$ fs mount fat /mp0
7. uart:~$ fs ls

### Relevant log output

### Board / SoC
- Board: `frdm_mcxw72`

### Problem description
After flashing **any** sample application to `frdm_mcxw72`, subsequent debug/flash attempts via SWD stop working. To recover, I must switch the board to **ISP mode** and **erase** it. This happens with both **LinkServer** and **J-Link**.

### Steps to reproduce

```sh
west flash --runner linkserver

# run flash again
```

### Observed behavior

### Expected behavior

### Workaround
- Switch the board to ISP mode and erase the device. After that, flashing/debugging works again.

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104845.body:
### Summary

Subsystems that store a socket address value (as opposed to merely passing one through) should use `struct net_sockaddr_storage`, which is guaranteed to be large enough to hold any supported address family.

Several subsystems instead use `struct net_sockaddr`, which should be used as a function interface type or a hand-rolled union, which duplicates the same pattern that `struct net_sockaddr_storage` is designed to replace.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Any struct field or static/local variable whose purpose is to _store_ a socket address of potentially more than one address family should be typed as `struct net_sockaddr_storage`. The stored value can then be accessed through a pointer cast to the appropriate concrete type after inspecting `ss_family`, the same way the POSIX socket layer already does it.

~~Hand-rolled unions of `struct net_sockaddr_in`/`struct net_sockaddr_in6`/`struct net_sockaddr` should be removed in favor of a single `struct net_sockaddr_storage` field.~~

### Alternatives

Keep as is.

### Additional Context

_No response_
zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104845.title: net: Use `struct net_sockaddr_storage` for storing socket addresses

### Summary

If I want to encode a struct like this `struct json_null {char *value;}` with null as a value, I'd like to use descriptor like this `JSON_OBJ_DESCR_PRIM(struct json_null, value, JSON_TOK_NULL)` and get `{"value":null}` as a result.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A solution would be to add an extra case at the [json.c on line 1734](https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/014b2db1f753764b69970eb13c5a722b525c96ec/lib/utils/json.c#L1733) like this 
`case JSON_TOK_NULL:
	return append_bytes("null", 4, data);`

### Alternatives

_No response_

_No response_

zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr#104974.body: ### Summary There are some issues in how the doxygen documentation is getting generated under stepper area: 1. The word interface is redundant and needs to be dropped from the group names 2. There is a free floating stepper.h under stepper area, this is because the file is placed in wrong group. --- The ideal structure would be something like in https://docs.zephyrproject.org/latest/doxygen/html/group__can__interface.html ### Describe the solution you'd like 1. Drop _interface and Interface from https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/a15d30472ffea30b34824c0f7147e9a5c74f327c/include/zephyr/drivers/stepper/stepper.h#L29 https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/a15d30472ffea30b34824c0f7147e9a5c74f327c/include/zephyr/drivers/stepper/stepper_ctrl.h#L17 https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/a15d30472ffea30b34824c0f7147e9a5c74f327c/include/zephyr/drivers/stepper/stepper_ctrl.h#L8 2. Replace `stepper_interface` with `stepper_hw_driver` https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/a15d30472ffea30b34824c0f7147e9a5c74f327c/include/zephyr/drivers/stepper/stepper.h#L9 ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional Context _No response_
